action: Listen to Kannada vowel sounds. Notice how short and long vowels sound different.
description: Introduction to Kannada's 5 vowel pairs — each has a short and long version that changes word meaning.
context: Kannada has 10 core vowels: 5 short and 5 long. Getting these right is the foundation of pronunciation. English doesn't distinguish vowel length, so this is new for most learners.
constraint_note: Just listen for now. Don't try to repeat yet. Let your ear learn the difference.
example: Short 'a' (ಅ) as in 'anna' (rice) vs long 'aa' (ಆ) as in 'aane' (elephant). Same starting sound, different length, different word.
narrator_action: Listen to Kannada vowel sounds. Notice how short and long vowels sound different.

action: Listen to Kannada retroflex sounds. These are made with the tongue curled back — English doesn't have them.
description: Retroflex consonants are the most distinctive Kannada sounds for English speakers. The tongue tip curls back to touch the roof of the mouth.
context: Retroflex sounds exist in all Dravidian languages. They're the reason Kannada sounds different from European languages. Mastering these makes you sound natural.
constraint_note: Listen to the difference between the dental (tongue on teeth) and retroflex (tongue curled back) versions.
example: Dental 'ta' (ತ) — tongue touches teeth, like English 'th'. Retroflex 'ṭa' (ಟ) — tongue curls back, no English equivalent.
narrator_action: Listen to Kannada retroflex sounds. These are made with the tongue curled back — English doesn't have them.

action: Listen to aspirated vs unaspirated consonants. Aspirated sounds have a puff of air; unaspirated don't.
description: Kannada distinguishes between consonants with and without a breath of air. English speakers often don't notice this difference.
context: Hold your hand in front of your mouth. Say 'pin' — you feel air. Say 'spin' — less air. Kannada treats these as completely different sounds that change word meanings.
constraint_note: Listen for the puff of air in the aspirated versions. It's subtle but Kannada speakers hear it clearly.
example: 'ka' (ಕ) in 'kaapi' (coffee) vs 'kha' (ಖ) in 'khali' (empty). The 'kh' has a breath of air after it.
narrator_action: Listen to aspirated vs unaspirated consonants. Aspirated sounds have a puff of air; unaspirated don't.

action: Listen to the rhythm of Kannada. Notice how stress falls on the first syllable and sentences have a musical quality.
description: Kannada has a distinct rhythmic pattern — stress typically falls on the first syllable, and sentences flow with a gentle rise-and-fall pattern.
context: English is stress-timed (some syllables are longer). Kannada is more syllable-timed (each syllable gets roughly equal time). This gives Kannada its characteristic even, flowing rhythm.
constraint_note: Listen to each phrase 2-3 times. Don't focus on meaning — focus on the RHYTHM. Tap along if it helps.
example: NA-mas-kaa-ra (stress on first syllable). HE-gi-ddee-ra (stress on first). CHEN-na-gi-ddi-ni (stress on first).
narrator_action: Listen to the rhythm of Kannada. Notice how stress falls on the first syllable and sentences have a musical quality.

action: Listen to minimal pairs — words that sound almost the same but mean different things. Can you hear the difference?
description: Minimal pairs train your ear to distinguish sounds that matter in Kannada. These are words where one sound change flips the meaning.
context: This is the most effective ear training technique in linguistics. By hearing two similar words back-to-back, your brain learns to notice the difference that native speakers hear automatically.
constraint_note: Listen to each pair 3 times. First time: just listen. Second time: try to hear the difference. Third time: confirm you can tell them apart.
example: 'ಹಾಲು (haalu)' means milk. 'ಹಾಳು (haaḷu)' means ruined. The only difference is 'l' vs 'ḷ' (retroflex l).

pairs.difference: short o vs long oo, final vowel

narrator_action: Listen to minimal pairs — words that sound almost the same but mean different things. Can you hear the difference?

action: Listen to how Kannada words blend together in natural speech. Words connect differently than in English.
description: In natural Kannada, words flow into each other. Endings change, sounds merge, and the rhythm shifts. This is why textbook Kannada sounds different from street Kannada.
context: Every language has connected speech patterns. Learning these early prevents the shock of 'I learned Kannada but can't understand anyone.' Real Kannada sounds faster and smoother than word-by-word pronunciation.
constraint_note: Listen to the slow version first, then the natural speed version. Notice how sounds merge and shorten.
example: 'Hegiddira' in textbook form is 4 clear syllables. In natural speech it often sounds like 'Hegdira' — faster, smoother.

examples.note: In casual speech, often shortened to 'hegdira' or even 'hegdri'
Flows as one phrase: 'ondu-kaafi-kodi' with no pauses

narrator_action: Listen to how Kannada words blend together in natural speech. Words connect differently than in English.

action: Listen to how Kannada intonation changes meaning. The same words can be a statement, question, or exclamation depending on tone.
description: Kannada uses rising intonation for questions and falling intonation for statements — similar to English but with its own patterns.
context: Intonation is the melody of language. Getting it right makes you understood even when your words aren't perfect. Wrong intonation can turn a polite request into a rude demand.
constraint_note: Listen to each phrase three ways: as a statement (flat/falling), as a question (rising), and as an exclamation (sharp rise then fall).
example: 'Chennagi' (good) — flat tone = statement. Rising tone = 'Good?' (asking). Sharp emphasis = 'Good!' (impressed).
narrator_action: Listen to how Kannada intonation changes meaning. The same words can be a statement, question, or exclamation depending on tone.

action: Listen to Ramu's first morning in Bengaluru. Learn greetings and coffee-ordering words.
description: Story 1: Ramu has just moved to Bengaluru from Delhi. He walks to the neighborhood shop for his first coffee. Learn: Namaskara, angadi, mane, kaapi, bisi, sakkare, haal, beku, kodi, dhanyavada.
context: This is your first Kannada story. Don't try to memorize — just listen and absorb. The Kannada words are explained in context. By the end, you'll recognize 10 survival words.
constraint_note: Listen to the full story first. Then answer the questions from memory — don't re-read.
example: After this story you'll know how to order coffee: 'Ondu bisi kaapi kodi' — give one hot coffee.

segments.text: Ramu moved to Bengaluru last week. This morning, he walks to the angadi — the small shop — near his mane, his house.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu moved to Bengaluru last week. This morning, he walks to the small shop near his house.
segments.narrator_after: The first word means shop. The second means house.

segments.text: He sees the shopkeeper and says: Namaskara! That's how you say hello in Kannada.
segments.narrator_before: He sees the shopkeeper and greets him.
segments.narrator_after: That's how you say hello in Kannada.

segments.text: Ramu wants coffee. He says: Ondu bisi kaapi kodi. That means: give one hot coffee. Ondu is one. Bisi is hot. Kaapi is coffee. Kodi means give.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu wants coffee. He tells the shopkeeper.
segments.narrator_after: That means: give one hot coffee.

segments.text: The shopkeeper asks: Sakkare beku? That means: do you want sugar? Sakkare is sugar. Beku means want or need.
segments.narrator_before: The shopkeeper asks a question.
segments.narrator_after: Do you want sugar?

segments.text: Ramu says: Haudu, swalpa sakkare, mattu swalpa haal. Yes, a little sugar, and a little milk. Haudu means yes. Swalpa means a little. Haal is milk.
segments.audio_text: ಹೌದು, ಸ್ವಲ್ಪ ಸಕ್ಕರೆ, ಮತ್ತು ಸ್ವಲ್ಪ ಹಾಲು.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu replies.
segments.narrator_after: Yes, a little sugar, and a little milk.

segments.text: The coffee is ready. Ramu takes a sip. Tumba chennagide! Very good! He smiles and says: Dhanyavada! Thank you!
segments.narrator_before: The coffee is ready. Ramu takes a sip and smiles.
segments.narrator_after: Very good! Thank you!

questions.prompt: How do you say 'one hot coffee' in Kannada?
questions.narrator_before: How do you say 'one hot coffee' in Kannada?
questions.narrator_after: The answer is: one hot coffee.

action: Recall drill: greetings and basic responses. Build up from single words to full exchanges.
description: Graduated recall drill for greetings. You'll hear a prompt, pause to recall, then hear the answer. Each step builds on the previous one.
context: This is Pimsleur-style active recall. The pause is where learning happens — your brain searching for the word strengthens the memory trace. Don't skip the pause.
constraint_note: Say your answer OUT LOUD during the pause. Thinking it isn't enough — speaking activates motor memory.
example: Prompt: 'How do you say hello?' → You say: 'Namaskara' → Audio confirms: 'Namaskara'

prompts.prompt: Hello in Kannada is Namaskara. Say it: Namaskara.
Thank you is Dhanyavada. Say it: Dhanyavada.

prompts.prompt: How do you say hello?
How are you is Hegiddira. Say it.
prompts.narrator_before: How do you say: How are you?

prompts.prompt: I'm fine is Chennagiddini. Say it.
prompts.narrator_before: How do you say: I'm fine?

prompts.prompt: How do you say thank you?
prompts.narrator_before: How do you say thank you?

prompts.prompt: Someone says Hegiddira to you. How do you respond?
prompts.narrator_before: Someone greets you with: how are you? How do you respond?

prompts.prompt: Please is Dayavittu. Sorry or excuse me is Kshamisi. Say please.
Say excuse me.
Now put it together. Greet someone, ask how they are, and say thank you.
prompts.narrator_before: Now put it together. Greet someone, ask how they are, and say thank you.

prompts.prompt: How do you say hello?
How do you say how are you?
prompts.narrator_before: How do you say how are you?

narrator_action: Recall drill: greetings and basic responses. Build up from single words to full exchanges.

action: Listen to Ramu's breakfast at Meera's darshini. Learn food words and numbers.
description: Story 2: Ramu discovers the darshini — a quick-service restaurant unique to Karnataka. He orders breakfast and learns food vocabulary. Learn: darshini, idli, dosa, eradu, mooru, oota, eshtu, rupaayi, tumba, chennagi.
context: Darshinis are everywhere in Bengaluru — standing-only restaurants where you eat fast and cheap. Knowing food words is survival vocabulary.
constraint_note: Listen for the numbers. After the story, you should know ondu (1), eradu (2), mooru (3).
example: After this story you'll know how to order: 'Eradu idli, ondu dosa kodi' — give two idlis and one dosa.

segments.text: Ramu's landlady Lakshmi tells him: go to Meera's darshini for oota — for food. A darshini is a quick restaurant where you eat standing up.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu's landlady Lakshmi tells him to go to a quick restaurant for food.
segments.narrator_after: Go to the restaurant for food.

segments.text: Ramu walks in and sees the menu. Meera asks: Enu beku? What do you want? Enu means what.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu walks in and sees the menu. The owner Meera asks him.
segments.narrator_after: What do you want?

segments.text: Ramu says: Eradu idli kodi. Give two idlis. Eradu means two.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu orders.
segments.narrator_after: Give two idlis.

segments.text: Then he adds: Mattu ondu dosa. And one dosa. Mattu means and.
segments.narrator_before: Then he adds.
segments.narrator_after: And one dosa.

segments.text: The food arrives. Ramu eats and says: Tumba chennagi! Very good! Meera smiles.
segments.narrator_before: The food arrives. Ramu eats and tells Meera.
segments.narrator_after: Very good!

segments.text: Ramu asks: Eshtu? How much? Meera says: Mooru-vattu rupaayi. Thirty rupees. Mooru is three.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu asks how much it costs. Meera tells him.
segments.narrator_after: How much? Thirty rupees.

segments.text: Ramu pays and says: Dhanyavada, tumba chennagi oota! Thank you, very good food!
segments.narrator_before: Ramu pays and thanks her.
segments.narrator_after: Thank you, very good food!

questions.narrator_after: The answer is: A quick-service restaurant (eat standing up)

action: Recall drill: numbers and ordering. Build from numbers to ordering food and asking prices.
description: Graduated recall drill for numbers 1-5 and ordering patterns. Builds up: number → number + item → full order → asking price.
context: Numbers are the most immediately useful vocabulary. You'll use them within hours of learning them — at every shop, restaurant, and auto ride.
constraint_note: Say each number and phrase out loud. Speed matters less than accuracy.
example: Prompt: 'How do you say one coffee?' → You say: 'Ondu kaapi' → Audio confirms.

action: Circling drill: 'X beku' — I want X. Hear the same structure asked 6 different ways.
description: TPRS circling exercise for the 'beku' (want/need) structure. You'll hear the same pattern repeated as statements, yes/no questions, either/or questions, and wh-questions. Massive repetition that doesn't feel repetitive.
context: Circling is the most effective technique for making a structure automatic. By hearing 'beku' in 10+ different sentences, your brain stops translating and starts understanding directly.
constraint_note: Answer the questions out loud — even if it's just haudu or illa. Participation makes circling work.
example: 'Ramuge kaapi beku' → 'Does Ramu want coffee?' → 'Does Ramu want tea or coffee?' → 'What does Ramu want?'

prompts.text: Ramu wants coffee. Ramuge kaapi beku.
Does Ramu want coffee? Ramuge kaapi beku-aa?
Does Ramu want tea? Ramuge chai beku-aa?
prompts.audio_text: ರಾಮುಗೆ ಚಾಯ್ ಬೇಕಾ? ಇಲ್ಲ! ಕಾಫಿ ಬೇಕು.

prompts.text: Does Ramu want coffee or tea? Ramuge kaapi beku-aa, chai beku-aa?
prompts.audio_text: ರಾಮುಗೆ ಕಾಫಿ ಬೇಕಾ, ಚಾಯ್ ಬೇಕಾ? ಕಾಫಿ ಬೇಕು!
prompts.narrator_before: Does Ramu want coffee or tea?

prompts.text: What does Ramu want? Ramuge enu beku?
Who wants coffee? Yarige kaapi beku?
Priya wants water. Priyage neeru beku.
Does Priya want water? Priyage neeru beku-aa?
What does Priya want? Priyage enu beku?
Does Priya want water or coffee? Priyage neeru beku-aa, kaapi beku-aa?
prompts.audio_text: ಪ್ರಿಯಾಗೆ ನೀರು ಬೇಕಾ, ಕಾಫಿ ಬೇಕಾ? ನೀರು ಬೇಕು!
prompts.narrator_before: Does Priya want water or coffee?

action: Listen to Ramu's first auto ride. Learn transport phrases and negotiating.
description: Story 3: Ramu needs to get to work. He takes an auto rickshaw with Venkatesh the driver. Learn: auto, hogi, banni, yelli, eshtu, nillisi, bala, edakke, hattira, doora.
context: Auto rickshaws are the lifeline of Bengaluru. Knowing how to tell the driver where to go and negotiate the fare is essential survival Kannada.
constraint_note: Pay attention to the direction words: bala (right), edakke (left), nere (straight). You'll use these every day.
example: After this story you'll know: 'Majestic-ge hogi. Eshtu?' — Go to Majestic. How much?

segments.text: Ramu needs to go to work. He sees an auto rickshaw. The driver is Venkatesh. Ramu says: Namaskara! Majestic-ge hogi. That means: go to Majestic. Hogi means go.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu needs to go to work. He sees an auto rickshaw. The driver is Venkatesh.
segments.narrator_after: Hello! Go to Majestic.

segments.text: Venkatesh asks: Yelli? Where exactly? Yelli means where.
segments.narrator_before: Venkatesh asks where exactly.
segments.narrator_after: Where?

segments.text: Ramu says: Bus stand hattira. Near the bus stand. Hattira means near.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu answers.
segments.narrator_after: Near the bus stand.

segments.text: Ramu asks: Eshtu aagutte? How much will it be? Venkatesh says: Nooru rupaayi. Hundred rupees.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu asks the price. Venkatesh answers.
segments.narrator_after: How much will it be? Hundred rupees.

segments.text: Ramu says: Tumba jaasti! Too much! Swalpa kammi maadi. Reduce a little. Jaasti means too much. Kammi means less.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu thinks it's too expensive.
segments.narrator_after: Too much! Reduce a little.

segments.text: Venkatesh laughs: Sari, embattu rupaayi. Okay, eighty rupees. Sari means okay. They drive off.
segments.narrator_before: Venkatesh agrees to a lower price. They drive off.
segments.narrator_after: Okay, eighty rupees.

segments.text: On the way, Venkatesh says: Bala tiru. Turn right. Then: Edakke tiru. Turn left. Bala is right. Edakke is left.
segments.narrator_before: On the way, Venkatesh gives directions.
segments.narrator_after: Turn right. Turn left.

segments.text: They arrive. Ramu says: Illi nillisi. Stop here. Nillisi means stop. Dhanyavada, Venkatesh!
segments.narrator_before: They arrive. Ramu tells him to stop.
segments.narrator_after: Stop here. Thank you!

action: Recall drill: question words. Learn to ask what, where, when, how much, and who.
description: Graduated recall drill for the 6 essential question words: enu (what), yelli (where), yavaga (when), eshtu (how much), yaru (who), hege (how).
context: Question words are force multipliers — each one unlocks hundreds of possible questions. With just 6 words you can ask about anything.
constraint_note: Try to recall each question word before the answer plays. The struggle to remember is what builds the memory.
example: Prompt: 'How do you ask where?' → You say: 'Yelli?' → Audio confirms.

How much is Eshtu. When is Yavaga. Say: how much, when.

action: Circling drill: 'X-ge hogi' — go to X. Practice destinations and directions.
description: TPRS circling exercise for the 'hogi' (go) structure with destinations. Circles through: angadi (shop), mane (house), darshini, office, bus stand.
context: You'll say 'hogi' dozens of times a day in Bengaluru — to auto drivers, when making plans, when giving directions. This drill makes it automatic.
constraint_note: Listen for the '-ge' suffix on destinations. It means 'to' — angadi-ge means 'to the shop'.
example: 'Angadige hogi' (go to the shop) → 'Yelli hogi?' (where to go?) → 'Manege hogi-aa?' (go home?)

action: Listen to Ramu meeting his colleague Priya. Learn how to introduce yourself.
description: Story 4: Ramu arrives at work and meets Priya. They introduce themselves in Kannada. Learn: hesaru, nanna, nimma, yelli-inda, kelsa, hegiddira, chennagiddini, gottu, swalpa swalpa.
context: Introductions happen constantly when you're new somewhere. These phrases let you tell people your name, where you're from, and what you do.
constraint_note: After listening, practice introducing yourself: Nanna hesaru ___, naanu ___-inda, naanu ___ kelsa maadthini.
example: After this story you'll know: 'Nanna hesaru Ramu. Naanu Delhi-inda' — My name is Ramu. I'm from Delhi.

segments.text: Ramu reaches his office. A woman walks up and says: Namaskara! Nanna hesaru Priya. My name is Priya. Hesaru means name. Nanna means my.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu reaches his office. A woman walks up and introduces herself.
segments.narrator_after: Hello! My name is Priya.

segments.text: Ramu replies: Namaskara! Nanna hesaru Ramu. My name is Ramu.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu introduces himself back.
segments.narrator_after: Hello! My name is Ramu.

segments.text: Priya asks: Nimma ooru yelli? Where is your town? Nimma means your. Ooru means town or place.
segments.narrator_before: Priya asks where he's from.
segments.narrator_after: Where is your town?

segments.text: Ramu says: Naanu Delhi-inda. I'm from Delhi. Inda means from.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu tells her.
segments.narrator_after: I'm from Delhi.

segments.text: Priya asks: Kannada gottha? Do you know Kannada? Gottha means do you know.
segments.narrator_before: Priya asks if he knows Kannada.
segments.narrator_after: Do you know Kannada?

segments.text: Ramu laughs: Swalpa swalpa! A little bit! Priya says: Paravagilla, kalithukollthira! No problem, you'll learn! Paravagilla means no problem.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu laughs. Priya encourages him.
segments.narrator_after: A little bit! No problem, you'll learn!

segments.text: Priya says: Hegiddira means how are you. Ramu tries: Hegiddira? Priya smiles: Chennagiddini! I'm fine!
segments.narrator_before: Priya teaches him a greeting.
segments.narrator_after: How are you? I'm fine!

action: Recall drill: core verbs. Learn to say want, go, come, give, and stop.
description: Graduated recall drill for the 5 most useful Kannada verbs: beku (want/need), hogi (go), banni (come), kodi (give), nillisi (stop). Builds to full sentences.
context: These 5 verbs cover most of what you need to communicate as a beginner. Combined with nouns and question words, they let you express needs, give directions, and make requests.
constraint_note: Verbs in Kannada usually come at the end of the sentence. Listen for the pattern: subject + object + verb.
example: Prompt: 'How do you say I want coffee?' → You say: 'Nanage kaapi beku'

prompts.prompt: Want or need is Beku. I want coffee: Nanage kaapi beku. Say it.

Come is Banni. Come here: Illi banni. Say it.

action: Circling drill: 'Eshtu?' — How much? Practice prices and quantities.
description: TPRS circling exercise for 'eshtu' (how much) in shopping and ordering contexts. Circles through prices, quantities, and bargaining.
context: Eshtu is the word you'll use most at markets, shops, and restaurants. This drill makes price conversations automatic.
constraint_note: Listen for the numbers in the answers. Try to catch the price before the English translation.
example: 'Kaapi eshtu?' (How much is coffee?) → 'Hattu rupaayi' (Ten rupees) → 'Tumba jaasti!' (Too much!)

prompts.text: Coffee costs ten rupees. Kaapige hattu rupaayi.
How much is the coffee? Kaapi eshtu?
Is the coffee ten rupees? Kaapi hattu rupaayi-aa?
Is the coffee twenty rupees? Kaapi ippattu rupaayi-aa?
prompts.audio_text: ಕಾಫಿ ಇಪ್ಪತ್ತು ರೂಪಾಯಿಯಾ? ಇಲ್ಲ! ಹತ್ತು ರೂಪಾಯಿ.

prompts.text: Dosa costs thirty rupees. Dosege mooru-vattu rupaayi.
How much is the dosa? Dose eshtu?
Is the dosa thirty or forty rupees?
prompts.audio_text: ದೋಸೆ ಮೂವತ್ತು ರೂಪಾಯಿಯಾ, ನಲವತ್ತು ರೂಪಾಯಿಯಾ? ಮೂವತ್ತು!
prompts.narrator_before: Is the dosa thirty or forty rupees?

prompts.text: What costs thirty rupees? Enu mooru-vattu rupaayi?
Ramu says: too much! Reduce a little. Tumba jaasti! Swalpa kammi maadi.
Total how much? Ottu eshtu?

narrator_action: Circling drill: 'Eshtu?' — How much? Practice prices and quantities.

action: Listen to Ramu getting lost in Jayanagar. Learn how to ask for directions.
description: Story 5: Ramu is trying to find a shop in Jayanagar but gets lost. He asks people for directions. Learn: yelli, hege, nere, bala, edakke, hattira, doora, mundhe, gottilla, sahaya maadi.
context: Getting lost is inevitable in a new city. These direction phrases will save you every time. Bengaluru locals are helpful — they just need you to ask in Kannada.
constraint_note: Focus on the direction words. Draw a mental map as you listen: straight, right, left, near, far.
example: After this story you'll know: 'Kshamisi, ___ yelli?' — Excuse me, where is ___?

segments.text: Ramu is in Jayanagar. He needs to find a medical shop — an aushadhi angadi. But he's lost. He doesn't know where it is. Gottilla — I don't know.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu is in Jayanagar. He needs to find a medical shop but he's lost.
segments.narrator_after: Medical shop. I don't know.

segments.text: He sees an old man and asks: Kshamisi, aushadhi angadi yelli? Excuse me, where is the medical shop? Kshamisi means excuse me.
segments.narrator_before: He sees an old man and asks for directions.
segments.narrator_after: Excuse me, where is the medical shop?

segments.text: The man says: Nere hogi. Go straight. Nere means straight. Hogi means go.
segments.narrator_before: The man tells him to go straight.
segments.narrator_after: Go straight.

segments.text: Then: Bala tiru. Turn right. Then: Swalpa mundhe hogi. Go a little forward. Mundhe means forward.
segments.narrator_before: Then gives more directions.
segments.narrator_after: Turn right. Go a little forward.

segments.text: The man adds: Angadi bala pakka ide. The shop is on the right side. Pakka means next to or side. Ide means is.
segments.narrator_before: The man adds where the shop is.
segments.narrator_after: The shop is on the right side.

segments.text: Ramu asks: Doora-aa? Is it far? The man says: Illa, hattira ide. No, it's nearby. Doora means far. Hattira means near.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu asks if it's far. The man reassures him.
segments.narrator_after: Is it far? No, it's nearby.

segments.text: Ramu follows the directions and finds the shop. He thinks: Kannada gottu — I know Kannada! Swalpa swalpa, but it works!
segments.narrator_before: Ramu follows the directions and finds the shop. He's proud.
segments.narrator_after: I know Kannada!

action: Recall drill: essential nouns and building full sentences. Combine everything from Tier 1.
description: Graduated recall drill combining nouns (neeru, kaapi, anna, mane, angadi, oota, auto) with verbs and question words into complete sentences.
context: This is the capstone drill for Tier 1. If you can produce these sentences from memory, you have survival Kannada. You can order food, take autos, ask directions, and greet people.
constraint_note: These are longer phrases. Take your time. Getting the word order right matters more than speed.
example: Prompt: 'You're at a darshini. Order food and ask the price.' → Full Kannada response.

prompts.prompt: Water is Neeru. House is Mane. Food is Oota. Say: water, house, food.
How do you say: give water please?

action: Circling drill: 'Yelli?' — Where? Practice asking about locations.
description: TPRS circling exercise for 'yelli' (where) with common locations: angadi, darshini, mane, bus stand, bathroom.
context: Yelli is your navigation word. Combined with place names, it gets you anywhere in Bengaluru. Locals will point and explain — you just need to ask.
constraint_note: Notice the answers use 'illi' (here), 'alli' (there), 'hattira' (near), 'doora' (far). These are your location words.
example: 'Darshini yelli?' (Where is the darshini?) → 'Alli!' (There!) → 'Hattira-aa?' (Is it near?)

prompts.text: Where is the darshini? Darshini yelli?
Is the darshini here? Darshini illi ide-aa?
prompts.audio_text: ದರ್ಶಿನಿ ಇಲ್ಲಿ ಇದೆಯಾ? ಇಲ್ಲ, ಅಲ್ಲಿ ಇದೆ!

prompts.text: Is the darshini near? Darshini hattira ide-aa?
prompts.audio_text: ದರ್ಶಿನಿ ಹತ್ತಿರ ಇದೆಯಾ? ಹೌದು, ಹತ್ತಿರ ಇದೆ.

prompts.text: Is the darshini near or far? Hattira-aa, doora-aa?
prompts.narrator_before: Is the darshini near or far?

prompts.text: Where is the bathroom? Bathroom yelli?
Where is the bus stand? Bus stand yelli?
prompts.audio_text: ಬಸ್ ಸ್ಟ್ಯಾಂಡ್ ಎಲ್ಲಿ? ನೇರ ಹೋಗಿ, ಎಡಕ್ಕೆ ತಿರು.

prompts.text: Is the bus stand far? Bus stand doora-aa?
prompts.audio_text: ಬಸ್ ಸ್ಟ್ಯಾಂಡ್ ದೂರಾ? ಹೌದು, ಸ್ವಲ್ಪ ದೂರ.

prompts.text: What is near? Enu hattira ide?
prompts.audio_text: ಏನು ಹತ್ತಿರ ಇದೆ? ದರ್ಶಿನಿ ಹತ್ತಿರ ಇದೆ.

prompts.text: Where is Ramu's house? Ramu mane yelli?
Where is the medical shop? Aushadhi angadi yelli?
prompts.audio_text: ಔಷಧಿ ಅಂಗಡಿ ಎಲ್ಲಿ? ನೇರ ಹೋಗಿ, ಬಲ ತಿರು.

narrator_action: Circling drill: 'Yelli?' — Where? Practice asking about locations.

action: Circling drill: 'X kodi' — Give X. Practice ordering with numbers.
description: TPRS circling exercise for 'kodi' (give) combined with numbers and food items. Circles through ordering at a darshini.
context: Kodi is the polite command form of 'give'. Combined with a number and a food item, it's your complete ordering phrase. This is the most practical drill in the set.
constraint_note: Try to answer before the audio does. The goal is automatic production — no translation in your head.
example: 'Ondu kaapi kodi' (give one coffee) → 'Eradu idli kodi' (give two idlis) → 'Enu kodi?' (give what?)

prompts.text: Ramu orders: one coffee please. Ondu kaapi kodi.
Does Ramu order one coffee? Ondu kaapi kodi-aa?
Does Ramu order two coffees? Eradu kaapi kodi-aa?
prompts.audio_text: ಎರಡು ಕಾಫಿ ಕೊಡಿಯಾ? ಇಲ್ಲ! ಒಂದು ಕಾಫಿ.

prompts.text: How many coffees? Eshtu kaapi?
What does Ramu order? Ramu enu order maadthane?
prompts.audio_text: ರಾಮು ಏನು ಆರ್ಡರ್ ಮಾಡ್ತಾನೆ? ಒಂದು ಕಾಫಿ!

prompts.text: Priya orders: two idlis and one dosa. Eradu idli mattu ondu dose kodi.
prompts.audio_text: ಎರಡು ಇಡ್ಲಿ ಮತ್ತು ಒಂದು ದೋಸೆ ಕೊಡಿ.
prompts.narrator_before: Priya orders: two idlis and one dosa.

prompts.text: What does Priya order? Priya enu order maadthale?
prompts.audio_text: ಪ್ರಿಯಾ ಏನು ಆರ್ಡರ್ ಮಾಡ್ತಾಳೆ? ಎರಡು ಇಡ್ಲಿ, ಒಂದು ದೋಸೆ!

prompts.text: How many idlis? Eshtu idli?
Does Priya order two dosas or two idlis?
prompts.audio_text: ಪ್ರಿಯಾ ಎರಡು ದೋಸೆ ಕೊಡಿಯಾ, ಎರಡು ಇಡ್ಲಿ ಕೊಡಿಯಾ? ಎರಡು ಇಡ್ಲಿ!
prompts.narrator_before: Does Priya order two dosas or two idlis?

prompts.text: Now you order: three dosas and water. Mooru dose mattu neeru kodi.
prompts.narrator_before: Now you order: three dosas and water.

action: Listen to Ramu calling his mother. Learn family words and time expressions.
description: Story 6: Ramu calls his Amma in Delhi after his first week in Bengaluru. Learn: amma, appa, anna, akka, ivattu, naale, beligge, saanje, raatri, hege, kelsa, chennagi.
context: Phone calls home are where you first use time words naturally — 'I'll call tomorrow morning' or 'work was good today.' Family words are high-frequency because people always ask about your family.
constraint_note: Listen for time words: ivattu (today), naale (tomorrow), beligge (morning), saanje (evening). These show up in every conversation about plans.
example: After this story you'll know: 'Naale beligge phone maadthini' — I'll call tomorrow morning.

segments.text: It's saanje — evening — in Bengaluru. Ramu calls his amma — his mother — in Delhi.
segments.narrator_before: It's evening in Bengaluru. Ramu calls his mother in Delhi.
segments.narrator_after: Evening. Mother.

segments.text: Amma answers: Ramu! Hegiddiya? How are you? Ramu says: Chennagiddini, Amma. I'm fine, Mom.
segments.narrator_before: His mother answers. Ramu tells her he's fine.
segments.narrator_after: Ramu! How are you? I'm fine, Mom.

segments.text: Amma asks: Kelsa hege ide? How is work? Kelsa means work. Ramu says: Kelsa tumba chennagi ide. Work is very good.
segments.audio_text: ಕೆಲಸ ಹೇಗೆ ಇದೆ? ಕೆಲಸ ತುಂಬಾ ಚೆನ್ನಾಗಿ ಇದೆ.
segments.narrator_before: She asks about work. Ramu says it's going well.
segments.narrator_after: How is work? Work is very good.

segments.text: Amma asks: Oota sari aagthide-aa? Are you eating properly? Ramu says: Haudu! Ivattu beligge idli tinde. Yes! This morning I ate idli. Ivattu means today. Beligge means morning.
segments.audio_text: ಊಟ ಸರಿ ಆಗ್ತಿದೆಯಾ? ಹೌದು! ಇವತ್ತು ಬೆಳಿಗ್ಗೆ ಇಡ್ಲಿ ತಿಂದೆ.
segments.narrator_before: She asks if he's eating properly. Ramu reassures her.
segments.narrator_after: Are you eating properly? Yes! This morning I had breakfast.

segments.text: Ramu asks: Appa hegiddare? How is father? Appa means father. Amma says: Appa chennagiddare. Nimma akka naale barthale. Father is fine. Your elder sister is coming tomorrow. Akka means elder sister. Naale means tomorrow.
segments.audio_text: ಅಪ್ಪ ಹೇಗಿದ್ದಾರೆ? ಅಪ್ಪ ಚೆನ್ನಾಗಿದ್ದಾರೆ. ನಿಮ್ಮ ಅಕ್ಕ ನಾಳೆ ಬರ್ತಾಳೆ.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu asks about his father. His mother gives family news.
segments.narrator_after: How is father? Father is fine. Your sister is coming tomorrow.

segments.text: Ramu says: Tumba khushi! Very happy! Naale raatri phone maadthini. I'll call tomorrow night. Raatri means night.
segments.audio_text: ತುಂಬಾ ಖುಷಿ! ನಾಳೆ ರಾತ್ರಿ ಫೋನ್ ಮಾಡ್ತೀನಿ.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu is happy. He'll call again tomorrow night.
segments.narrator_after: Very happy! I'll call tomorrow night.

segments.text: Amma says: Sari, jagratha. Okay, take care. Ramu says: Hogi barthini, Amma. Goodbye, Mom. Hogi barthini literally means 'I'll go and come' — the Kannada way of saying goodbye.
segments.narrator_before: His mother says take care. Ramu says goodbye.
segments.narrator_after: Okay, take care. Goodbye, Mom.

constraint_note: Say each word out loud. Build up to ordering a complete meal.
example: 'What is rice?' → 'Anna' → 'Order rice with rasam and sambar' → 'Anna, saaru, huli kodi'

action: Listen to Ramu shopping at the weekend market with Lakshmi. Learn food and bargaining words.
description: Story 7: Lakshmi takes Ramu to the local market on Saturday. He learns to buy vegetables and bargain. Learn: tarkari, hannu, tomato, aalugadde, irulli, bele, kempu, hasiru, jaasti, kammi, kilo, sari.
context: Weekend market trips are a Bengaluru ritual. Knowing vegetable names and bargaining phrases makes you a local, not a tourist paying tourist prices.
constraint_note: Listen for color words — kempu (red) and hasiru (green) — they come up when describing vegetables and fruits.
example: After this story you'll know: 'Ondu kilo tomato kodi. Eshtu?' — Give one kilo tomatoes. How much?

segments.text: It's Saturday beligge — morning. Lakshmi says: Ramu, market-ge hogona! Let's go to the market! Hogona means let's go.
segments.narrator_before: It's Saturday morning. Lakshmi invites Ramu to the market.
segments.narrator_after: Ramu, let's go to the market!

segments.text: At the market, Lakshmi points: Idu tarkari angadi. This is the vegetable shop. Tarkari means vegetables.
segments.narrator_before: At the market, Lakshmi points to a shop.
segments.narrator_after: This is the vegetable shop.

segments.text: Lakshmi orders: Ondu kilo tomato kodi. Give one kilo tomatoes. Mattu ardhaa kilo aalugadde. And half a kilo potatoes. Aalugadde means potatoes.
segments.audio_text: ಒಂದು ಕಿಲೋ ಟೊಮ್ಯಾಟೊ ಕೊಡಿ. ಮತ್ತು ಅರ್ಧ ಕಿಲೋ ಆಲೂಗಡ್ಡೆ.
segments.narrator_before: Lakshmi orders vegetables.
segments.narrator_after: Give one kilo tomatoes. And half a kilo potatoes.

segments.text: The seller says: Tomato — nalavattu rupaayi kilo. Forty rupees per kilo. Lakshmi says: Tumba jaasti! Mooru-vattu kodi. Too much! Give for thirty.
segments.audio_text: ಟೊಮ್ಯಾಟೊ ನಲವತ್ತು ರೂಪಾಯಿ ಕಿಲೋ. ತುಂಬಾ ಜಾಸ್ತಿ! ಮೂವತ್ತು ಕೊಡಿ.
segments.narrator_before: The seller quotes a price. Lakshmi bargains.
segments.narrator_after: Tomatoes, forty rupees per kilo. Too much! Give for thirty.

segments.text: Ramu sees irulli — onions — and asks: Irulli eshtu? How much are onions? The seller says: Ippattu rupaayi. Twenty rupees.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu sees onions and asks the price.
segments.narrator_after: How much are onions? Twenty rupees.

segments.text: Lakshmi picks up some hasiru mirchi — green chillies — and kempu hannnu — red fruit. Hasiru means green. Kempu means red. Hannu means fruit.
segments.narrator_before: Lakshmi picks up green chillies and red fruit.
segments.narrator_after: Green chillies. Red fruit.

segments.text: Ramu asks: Ottu eshtu aayitu? What's the total? The seller counts: Nooru ippattu rupaayi. Hundred and twenty rupees. Lakshmi pays and says: Sari, dhanyavada!
segments.audio_text: ಒಟ್ಟು ಎಷ್ಟು ಆಯಿತು? ನೂರ ಇಪ್ಪತ್ತು ರೂಪಾಯಿ. ಸರಿ, ಧನ್ಯವಾದ!
segments.narrator_before: Ramu asks for the total. Lakshmi pays.
segments.narrator_after: What's the total? Hundred and twenty rupees. Okay, thank you!

context: Every auto ride is a mini Kannada lesson. These phrases make you self-sufficient in Bengaluru transport.
constraint_note: Imagine you're in an auto. Say each direction command with confidence.
example: 'How do you say I need to go?' → 'Hogbeku' → 'Go straight, turn right, stop here'

action: Listen to Ramu and Lakshmi on a rainy evening. Learn weather words and adjectives.
description: Story 8: It's raining heavily in Bengaluru. Ramu is stuck at home with Lakshmi. They talk about weather and feelings. Learn: mazhe, gaali, bisilu, tanna, bisi, chennagi, ketta, dodda, chikka, khushi, bejaar.
context: Bengaluru's weather is a constant conversation topic — sudden rains, pleasant mornings, hot afternoons. Adjectives let you describe things and express opinions, which makes conversations real.
constraint_note: Focus on the adjective pairs: bisi/tanna (hot/cold), dodda/chikka (big/small), chennagi/ketta (good/bad). Opposites are easier to remember together.
example: After this story you'll know: 'Ivattu tumba mazhe ide' — Today there's a lot of rain.

segments.text: Ramu looks outside. Tumba mazhe ide! There's a lot of rain! Mazhe means rain. The gaali — the wind — is strong too. Gaali means wind.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu looks outside. It's raining heavily. The wind is strong.
segments.narrator_after: There's a lot of rain! Wind.

segments.text: Lakshmi makes chai. She says: Tanna aagide, bisi chai kudi. It's become cold, drink hot tea. Tanna means cold. Bisi means hot.
segments.narrator_before: Lakshmi makes tea. She tells Ramu to drink.
segments.narrator_after: It's become cold, drink hot tea.

segments.text: Ramu takes the chai and says: Tumba chennagi ide! Very good! Lakshmi smiles: Bengaluru mazhe tumba chennagi. Bengaluru rain is very nice.
segments.audio_text: ತುಂಬಾ ಚೆನ್ನಾಗಿ ಇದೆ! ಬೆಂಗಳೂರು ಮಳೆ ತುಂಬಾ ಚೆನ್ನಾಗಿ.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu takes the tea and compliments it. Lakshmi talks about the rain.
segments.narrator_after: Very good! Bengaluru rain is very nice.

segments.text: Ramu says: Delhi-alli bisilu tumba jaasti. In Delhi the heat is too much. Bisilu means sun or heat. But Bengaluru hava chennagi ide. Bengaluru weather is nice. Hava means weather.
segments.audio_text: ದೆಹಲಿಯಲ್ಲಿ ಬಿಸಿಲು ತುಂಬಾ ಜಾಸ್ತಿ. ಬೆಂಗಳೂರು ಹವಾ ಚೆನ್ನಾಗಿ ಇದೆ.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu compares it to Delhi's heat.
segments.narrator_after: In Delhi the heat is too much. Bengaluru weather is nice.

segments.text: Lakshmi says: Mazhe dodda aagide. The rain has become heavy. Dodda means big. Swalpa hotte-alli nilluththe. It'll stop in a little while.
segments.audio_text: ಮಳೆ ದೊಡ್ಡ ಆಗಿದೆ. ಸ್ವಲ್ಪ ಹೊತ್ತಿನಲ್ಲಿ ನಿಲ್ಲುತ್ತೆ.
segments.narrator_before: Lakshmi says the rain has become heavy but will stop soon.
segments.narrator_after: The rain has become heavy. It'll stop in a little while.

segments.text: Ramu says: Naanu khushi. I'm happy. Mazhe, bisi chai, mattu chennagi company. Rain, hot tea, and good company. Lakshmi laughs: Neevu tumba olleya huduga! You're a very good boy!
segments.audio_text: ನಾನು ಖುಷಿ. ಮಳೆ, ಬಿಸಿ ಚಾಯ್, ಮತ್ತು ಚೆನ್ನಾಗಿ ಕಂಪನಿ. ನೀವು ತುಂಬಾ ಒಳ್ಳೆಯ ಹುಡುಗ!
segments.narrator_before: Ramu says he's happy with the rain, tea, and company.
segments.narrator_after: I'm happy. Rain, hot tea, and good company. You're a very good boy!

action: Recall drill: time words. Build from single words to talking about your schedule.
description: Graduated recall for time: ivattu, naale, ninne, beligge, madhyahna, saanje, raatri, ghante, nimisha, yavaga.
context: Time words turn isolated vocabulary into real conversations. 'I'll come tomorrow morning' is more useful than knowing 'tomorrow' and 'morning' separately.
constraint_note: Build full time expressions: ivattu beligge (this morning), naale saanje (tomorrow evening).
example: 'How do you say tomorrow morning?' → 'Naale beligge'

action: Recall drill: adjectives. Learn opposite pairs — good/bad, big/small, hot/cold, fast/slow.
description: Graduated recall for adjectives: chennagi/ketta, dodda/chikka, bisi/tanna, bega/nidhaana, jaasti/kammi, hosa/halaya.
context: Adjectives let you describe and compare. Without them you can only name things — with them you can have opinions.
constraint_note: Learn in pairs — opposites stick together in memory.
example: 'Good is chennagi. What is bad?' → 'Ketta'

Big is dodda. Small is chikka. Say: big, small.
Hot is bisi. Cold is tanna. Fast is bega. Slow is nidhaana.
What is the opposite of chennagi?
What is the opposite of dodda?
More or too much is jaasti. Less is kammi.

action: Circling drill: adjective + noun. Practice describing things.
description: TPRS circling for adjective-noun combinations: dodda mane, chikka angadi, bisi kaapi, tanna neeru, chennagi oota.
context: Adjective + noun is how you describe the world around you.
constraint_note: In Kannada, adjective comes before the noun, same as English.
example: 'Dodda mane' (big house) → 'Is the house big?' → 'Is the house big or small?'

Is Meera's darshini big or small?

action: Listen to Ramu becoming a regular at Meera's darshini. Learn the full menu and eating verbs.
description: Story 9: Ramu now goes to Meera's darshini every day. She teaches him the full menu. Learn: anna, saaru, huli, mosaru, uppittu, kesari bath, neeru, haal, chai, tinnu, kudi, beku, beda (don't want).
context: Food is the fastest way into any culture. Knowing the full darshini menu means you can eat like a local. Tinnu (eat) and kudi (drink) are verbs you'll use at every meal.
constraint_note: Notice the difference between 'beku' (want) and 'beda' (don't want). These two words handle all your food preferences.
example: After this story you'll know: 'Anna, saaru, huli kodi. Mosaru beda.' — Give rice, rasam, sambar. Don't want curd.

segments.text: Ramu walks into Meera's darshini. Meera smiles: Namaskara Ramu! Ivattu enu beku? Hello Ramu! What do you want today?
segments.narrator_before: Ramu walks into Meera's restaurant. She greets him.
segments.narrator_after: Hello Ramu! What do you want today?

segments.text: Ramu says: Ivattu oota beku. Today I want a meal. Meera says: Sari! Anna, saaru, huli, palya, mosaru. Rice, rasam, sambar, vegetable dish, curd. This is a standard Kannada meal.
segments.audio_text: ಇವತ್ತು ಊಟ ಬೇಕು. ಸರಿ! ಅನ್ನ, ಸಾರು, ಹುಳಿ, ಪಲ್ಯ, ಮೊಸರು.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu wants a full meal. Meera lists the dishes.
segments.narrator_after: Today I want a meal. Okay! Rice, rasam, sambar, vegetable dish, curd.

segments.text: Ramu asks: Saaru enu? What is saaru? Meera says: Saaru is rasam — a thin, spicy soup. You drink it with rice. Huli is sambar — thicker, with vegetables.
segments.audio_text: ಸಾರು ಏನು? ಸಾರು ರಸಂ. ಹುಳಿ ಸಾಂಬಾರ್.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu asks what one of the dishes is. Meera explains.
segments.narrator_after: What is rasam? Rasam is a thin spicy soup. Sambar.

segments.text: Ramu tries the food: Anna tumba chennagi ide! The rice is very good! Saaru bisi ide. The rasam is hot. He eats — tinnu — and drinks — kudi — water.
segments.audio_text: ಅನ್ನ ತುಂಬಾ ಚೆನ್ನಾಗಿ ಇದೆ! ಸಾರು ಬಿಸಿ ಇದೆ.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu tries the food and likes it.
segments.narrator_after: The rice is very good! The rasam is hot.

segments.text: Meera asks: Mosaru beku-aa? Want curd? Ramu says: Haudu, swalpa mosaru kodi. Yes, give a little curd. Mattu neeru kodi. And give water.
segments.audio_text: ಮೊಸರು ಬೇಕಾ? ಹೌದು, ಸ್ವಲ್ಪ ಮೊಸರು ಕೊಡಿ. ಮತ್ತು ನೀರು ಕೊಡಿ.
segments.narrator_before: Meera offers curd. Ramu also asks for water.
segments.narrator_after: Want curd? Yes, give a little curd. And give water.

segments.text: Meera asks: Bisi kaapi beku-aa, chai beku-aa? Want hot coffee or tea? Ramu says: Chai beda, kaapi kodi. Don't want tea, give coffee. Beda means don't want.
segments.audio_text: ಬಿಸಿ ಕಾಫಿ ಬೇಕಾ, ಚಾಯ್ ಬೇಕಾ? ಚಾಯ್ ಬೇಡ, ಕಾಫಿ ಕೊಡಿ.
segments.narrator_before: Meera asks about drinks. Ramu chooses coffee.
segments.narrator_after: Want hot coffee or tea? Don't want tea, give coffee.

segments.text: Ramu finishes and says: Meera, nimma oota tumba chennagi! Your food is very good! Naale kuda barthini. I'll come tomorrow too. Kuda means also or too.
segments.audio_text: ಮೀರಾ, ನಿಮ್ಮ ಊಟ ತುಂಬಾ ಚೆನ್ನಾಗಿ! ನಾಳೆ ಕೂಡ ಬರ್ತೀನಿ.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu finishes and compliments the food. He'll come back tomorrow.
segments.narrator_after: Meera, your food is very good! I'll come tomorrow too.

questions.narrator_after: The answer is: rasam (thin spicy soup)
The answer is: sambar (thick lentil soup with vegetables)
The answer is: no, or don't want.

action: Recall drill: family words. Build from names to talking about your family.

context: People in Karnataka always ask about family. Being able to say 'my mother is in Delhi' or 'I have one sister' makes conversations personal and warm.
constraint_note: Notice the Kannada family system: anna/akka (elder brother/sister) and tamma/tangi (younger brother/sister) — age matters.
example: 'How do you say my mother?' → 'Nanna amma'

action: Listen to Ramu's day trip to Mysuru by bus. Learn travel and direction vocabulary.
description: Story 10: Ramu takes a weekend bus trip to Mysuru with Priya. He learns bus travel phrases and distance vocabulary. Learn: bus, ticket, yavaga, hogbeku, barthini, doora, hattira, ghante, nidhaana, bega, mundhe, hinde.
context: Bus travel is how most people get around Karnataka. This story covers buying tickets, asking about timing, and the vocabulary of travel — essential for anyone exploring beyond Bengaluru.
constraint_note: This is a longer story with more vocabulary. Listen twice if needed. Focus on the travel phrases — you'll use them for any trip.
example: After this story you'll know: 'Mysuru-ge bus yavaga? Ticket eshtu?' — When is the bus to Mysuru? How much is the ticket?

segments.text: Saturday beligge. Priya says: Ramu, naale Mysuru-ge hogona! Tomorrow let's go to Mysuru! Ramu says: Tumba chennagi idea! Great idea!
segments.audio_text: ರಾಮು, ನಾಳೆ ಮೈಸೂರಿಗೆ ಹೋಗೋಣ! ತುಂಬಾ ಚೆನ್ನಾಗಿ ಐಡಿಯಾ!
segments.narrator_before: Priya suggests a trip to Mysuru. Ramu loves the idea.
segments.narrator_after: Ramu, let's go to Mysuru tomorrow! Very good idea!

segments.text: At the bus stand, Ramu asks: Mysuru-ge bus yavaga? When is the bus to Mysuru? Yavaga means when. The man says: Aidu nimisha-alli. In five minutes.
segments.narrator_before: At the bus stand, Ramu asks about the bus.
segments.narrator_after: When is the bus to Mysuru? In five minutes.

segments.text: Ramu asks: Ticket eshtu? How much is the ticket? The man says: Eradu ticket — mooru nooru rupaayi. Two tickets — three hundred rupees.
segments.audio_text: ಟಿಕೆಟ್ ಎಷ್ಟು? ಎರಡು ಟಿಕೆಟ್ ಮೂರು ನೂರು ರೂಪಾಯಿ.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu asks about the ticket price.
segments.narrator_after: How much is the ticket? Two tickets, three hundred rupees.

segments.text: On the bus, Ramu asks Priya: Mysuru doora-aa? Is Mysuru far? Priya says: Swalpa doora. Mooru ghante aagutte. A little far. It'll take three hours. Ghante means hour.
segments.audio_text: ಮೈಸೂರು ದೂರಾ? ಸ್ವಲ್ಪ ದೂರ. ಮೂರು ಗಂಟೆ ಆಗುತ್ತೆ.
segments.narrator_before: On the bus, Ramu asks if Mysuru is far.
segments.narrator_after: Is Mysuru far? A little far. It'll take three hours.

segments.text: The bus is slow. Ramu says: Bus tumba nidhaana! The bus is very slow! Nidhaana means slow. Priya laughs: Paravagilla, enjoy the view!
segments.narrator_before: The bus is slow. Ramu comments. Priya is relaxed.
segments.narrator_after: The bus is very slow! No problem!

segments.text: They arrive. Priya says: Mysuru bantu! Mysuru has arrived — we're here! Ramu looks around: Tumba sundhara! Very beautiful! Sundhara means beautiful.
segments.narrator_before: They arrive in Mysuru. Ramu is impressed.
segments.narrator_after: Mysuru has arrived! Very beautiful!

segments.text: Priya says: Mundhe palace ide. The palace is ahead. Mundhe means ahead or forward. Hogona! Let's go! Ramu says: Hogona!
segments.narrator_before: Priya points to the palace ahead.
segments.narrator_after: The palace is ahead. Let's go!

segments.text: At the end of the day, Ramu says: Ivattu tumba chennagi dina! Today was a very good day! Naanu khushi. I'm happy. Priya says: Naanu kuda! Me too!
segments.audio_text: ಇವತ್ತು ತುಂಬಾ ಚೆನ್ನಾಗಿ ದಿನ! ನಾನು ಖುಷಿ. ನಾನು ಕೂಡ!
segments.narrator_before: At the end of the day, Ramu reflects on a great day.
segments.narrator_after: Today was a very good day! I'm happy. Me too!

action: Recall drill: more verbs. Learn see, listen, eat, drink, speak, sleep, know, like.
description: Graduated recall for Tier 2 verbs: noodu (see), kelu (listen/ask), tinnu (eat), kudi (drink), mathaadu (speak), malagu (sleep), gottu (know), ishtaa (like).
context: These verbs cover daily actions. Combined with Tier 1 verbs (beku, hogi, banni, kodi, nillisi), you can express almost any basic need or action.
constraint_note: Kannada verbs come at the end of the sentence. Practice the pattern: subject + object + verb.
example: 'How do you say I eat rice?' → 'Naanu anna tinthini'

context: Hogbeku is how you express 'I need to go to X' — used constantly.
constraint_note: Answer out loud.
example: 'Nanage office-ge hogbeku' → 'Where do you need to go?' → 'Do you need to go now?'

action: Recall drill: pronouns and connectors. Learn I, you, he, she, we, they, and, but, or.
description: Graduated recall for pronouns: naanu, neevu, avanu, avalu, naavu, avaru. Connectors: mattu, aadre, athava. Possessives: nanna, nimma.
context: Pronouns are the glue of sentences. Without them you can't say who does what. Connectors let you join ideas into real thoughts.
constraint_note: Neevu is formal 'you'. Neenu is informal. Use neevu with strangers and elders.
example: 'I is naanu. How do you say my?' → 'Nanna'

My is nanna. Your is nimma. Say: my, your.
And is mattu. But is aadre. Or is athava.

constraint_note: Answer out loud.
example: 'Nimge Kannada gottha?' → 'Do you know Kannada?' → 'Swalpa swalpa!'

action: Circling drill: weather — 'mazhe/bisilu/gaali'. Practice describing weather.
description: TPRS circling for weather: mazhe (rain), bisilu (sun/heat), gaali (wind), tanna (cold), bisi (hot).
context: Weather is the universal small talk topic. In Bengaluru, it changes three times a day.
constraint_note: Answer out loud.
example: 'Ivattu mazhe ide' → 'Is there rain today?' → 'Is it raining or sunny?'

action: Context guessing: hear sentences with one unknown word. Guess the meaning from context.
description: 5 sentences using Tier 1 vocabulary with one new word each. The surrounding context gives you enough clues to guess the unknown word.
context: This is how you learn words in real life — from context, not from dictionaries. Krashen calls this i+1: input that's just slightly above your level.
constraint_note: Listen to the full sentence. Don't panic about the unknown word — use what you DO know to figure it out.
example: 'Ramu is very hungry. He goes to the darshini and orders oota.' What is oota? → food/meal

items.sentence: Ramu wakes up. It's beligge — morning. He wants bisi kaapi. But first he needs to snana maadu — he goes to the bathroom.
items.hint: What do you do in the bathroom every morning?
items.narrator_before: What do you do in the bathroom every morning?
items.narrator_after: That word means: bathe, or take a bath.

items.sentence: Lakshmi is making oota. The kitchen smells tumba chennagi. She says: oota tayaar aagide — come and tinnu!
items.hint: The food is made and she's calling Ramu to eat. What is the food?
items.narrator_before: The food is made and she's calling him to eat. What is the food?

items.sentence: Ramu sees a dodda naayi on the road. The naayi is barking loudly. Ramu walks around it.
items.hint: It's big, it's on the road, it barks. What is it?
items.narrator_before: It's big, it's on the road, it barks. What is it?

items.sentence: It's tumba bisilu today. Ramu is sweating. He says: nanage neeru beku, tumba baayarike aagide.
items.hint: It's very hot, he's sweating, he wants water. What is he feeling?
items.narrator_before: It's very hot, he's sweating, he wants water. What is he feeling?

items.sentence: Ramu goes to the angadi. He wants to kharidu maadu a new phone. The phone costs hattu saavira rupaayi.
items.hint: He's at a shop, he wants a phone, it costs money. What does he want to do?
items.narrator_before: He's at a shop, he wants a phone, it costs money. What does he want to do?
items.narrator_after: That word means: buy, or purchase.

narrator_action: Context guessing: hear sentences with one unknown word. Guess the meaning from context.

action: Context guessing: 5 more sentences with Tier 2 vocabulary. Guess unknown words from context.
description: 5 sentences using Tier 2 vocabulary with one new word each. Slightly harder — the context requires combining multiple known words.
context: Each sentence you decode this way teaches you a word you'll never forget — because you figured it out yourself, not because someone told you.
constraint_note: Use everything you know. The unknown word is always guessable from the words around it.
example: 'Naale Ramu akka barthale. Ramu tumba santhosha.' What is santhosha? → happy/happiness

items.sentence: Ramu's amma calls. She asks: oota aayitha? Ramu says: haudu, darshini-alli anna tinde. Amma says: mane-alli ade bisi oota maadkobeku.
items.hint: Amma wants Ramu to make food at home, not eat at the darshini. She wants him to do it...
items.narrator_before: His mother wants Ramu to cook at home instead of eating out. She wants him to learn.

items.sentence: Ivattu Ramu kelsa-alli tumba dhanikha aagide. He worked from beligge to raatri. Now he wants to malagu.
items.hint: He worked from morning to night. How does he feel?
items.narrator_before: He worked from morning to night. How does he feel?

items.sentence: Priya says: naale chutti ide! No office. Ramu asks: enu maadthiya? Priya says: mane-alli vishraanthi.
items.hint: No office tomorrow. It's a day off. What is it?
items.narrator_before: No office tomorrow. It's a day off. What is it?

items.sentence: At the market, the seller says: ivattu tomato tumba taajaaa ide. Ninne-du tomato halaya aagittu.
items.hint: Today's tomatoes are this, but yesterday's were old. What are today's?
items.narrator_before: Today's tomatoes are this, but yesterday's were old. What are today's?

items.sentence: Ramu is at the bus stand. Bus tumba janarige tumbide. He can't get in. He waits for the mundina bus.
items.hint: This bus is full. He waits for the ___ bus. What comes after this one?
items.narrator_before: This bus is full. He waits for the ___ bus. What comes after this one?

narrator_action: Context guessing: 5 more sentences with Tier 2 vocabulary. Guess unknown words from context.

action: Context guessing: mixed situations. Guess unknown words from everyday Bengaluru scenarios.
description: 5 sentences mixing Tier 1 and Tier 2 vocabulary in realistic situations. Each has one new word to guess from context.
context: These scenarios are things you'd actually encounter in Bengaluru — at shops, in autos, at home. The unknown word is always guessable.
constraint_note: Read the full sentence before guessing. The answer is always in the context.
example: 'Ramu wants to sit but there's no kurchi.' What is kurchi? → chair

items.sentence: Ramu is at the darshini. He finishes his oota and wants to wash his kai — his hands. He asks: kai toluva yelli? Where can I wash hands?
items.hint: He finished eating and wants to clean his hands. What does he want to do?
items.narrator_before: He finished eating and wants to clean his hands. What does he want to do?

items.sentence: Lakshmi says: Ramu, baagilu muchi. The gaali is coming inside. Ramu closes the baagilu.
items.hint: Wind is coming inside. Ramu closes it. What is it?
items.narrator_before: Wind is coming inside. Ramu closes it. What is it?

items.sentence: Priya asks: Nimage yeshtu vayassu? Ramu says: Nanage ippattu-entu vayassu. Twenty-eight.
items.hint: Priya asks a number question. Ramu answers twenty-eight. What is she asking about?
items.narrator_before: Priya asks a number question. Ramu answers twenty-eight. What is she asking about?

items.sentence: It's raatri. Ramu is very dhanikha. He says: nanage nidde barthide. He goes to malagu.
items.hint: It's night, he's tired, he's going to sleep. What's coming to him?
items.narrator_before: It's night, he's tired, he's going to sleep. What's coming to him?

items.sentence: Venkatesh says: Ivattu traffic tumba jaasti. Normally ippattu nimisha, but ivattu ondu ghante aagutte.
items.hint: Usually 20 minutes but today 1 hour. What's causing the delay?
items.narrator_before: Usually 20 minutes but today 1 hour. What's causing the delay?
items.narrator_after: traffic means traffic (same word in Kannada!)

action: Recall drill: places. Learn darshini, aspathre, angadi, shale, devaalaya, and location words.

context: Knowing place names lets you ask for directions and understand answers. Combined with yelli and hattira/doora, you can navigate anywhere.
constraint_note: Practice asking 'X yelli?' for each place.
example: 'Hospital is aspathre. Ask where is the hospital?' → 'Aspathre yelli?'

action: Shadow these greeting phrases. Listen, then repeat at the same time as the audio.
description: Shadowing exercise for greetings: Namaskara, Hegiddira, Chennagiddini, Dhanyavada, Hogi barthini. Each phrase at slow, normal, and fast speed.
context: Shadowing means speaking AT THE SAME TIME as the audio — not after. This trains your mouth muscles and rhythm. Don't worry about perfection, match the flow.
constraint_note: First listen once. Then shadow along. Try to match the speed and rhythm, not just the words.
example: [Slow] 'Na-mas-kaa-ra' → [Normal] 'Namaskara' → [Fast] 'Namskara'
narrator_action: Shadow these greeting phrases. Listen, then repeat at the same time as the audio.

constraint_note: Focus on Ramu's lines only. Let Meera's lines wash over you.
example: Meera: Enu beku? → You: Ivattu oota beku. Anna, saaru, huli kodi.

constraint_note: Say your answer OUT LOUD before the Kannada plays.
example: 'Say hello and ask how they are' → You say: 'Namaskara! Hegiddira?'

narrator_action: Production drill: greetings and introductions. Hear English, say Kannada.

description: Learn 10 essential Kannada greetings and responses. Write each in Kannada script and practice saying them aloud.
action: Learn these 10 greetings: Namaskara, Hegiddira, Chennagiddini, Dhanyavada, Shubha Dina, Banni, Hogi Banni, Olledu, Sari, Illa.
context: Greetings are the first thing people notice. Using Kannada greetings — even basic ones — immediately changes how people respond to you in Karnataka.
constraint_note: Write each in Kannada script if you can, or transliteration. Say each one aloud 3 times. Practice the response pairs together.
example: ನಮಸ್ಕಾರ (Namaskara) = Hello/Greetings. ಹೇಗಿದ್ದೀರ? (Hegiddira?) = How are you? ಚೆನ್ನಾಗಿದ್ದೀನಿ (Chennagiddini) = I'm fine.

description: Learn 8 phrases for shopping and bargaining in Kannada. These are essential for market and shop interactions.
action: Learn: Idhara bele eshtu? (How much is this?), Tumba jaasti (Too expensive), Swalpa kammi maadi (Reduce a little), Bere banna idya? (Other color available?), Kodi (Give me), Beda (Don't want), Chennaagide (It's nice), Bill kodi (Give the bill).
context: Shopping phrases are immediately usable. Even basic Kannada at a shop changes the interaction — shopkeepers respond differently when you try.
constraint_note: Practice each phrase aloud. Role-play a shop interaction: point at something, ask the price, negotiate, decide.
example: ಇದರ ಬೆಲೆ ಎಷ್ಟು? (Idhara bele eshtu?) = How much is this? ತುಂಬಾ ಜಾಸ್ತಿ (Tumba jaasti) = Too much/expensive.
narrator_action: Learn: Idhara bele eshtu? (How much is this?), Tumba jaasti (Too expensive), Swalpa kammi maadi (Reduce a little), Bere banna idya? (Other color available?), Kodi (Give me), Beda (Don't want), Chennaagide (It's nice), Bill kodi (Give the bill).

description: Learn 8 phrases for auto-rickshaw rides. These are the most common transport interactions in Karnataka.
action: Learn: ___ ge hogbeku (I need to go to ___), Eshtu aagutte? (How much will it be?), Meter haaki (Put the meter), Tumba doora (Too far), Illi nilsi (Stop here), Bala tiru (Turn right), Edakke tiru (Turn left), Nidhaanavaagi hogi (Go slowly).
context: Auto rides are where most non-Kannada speakers first need the language. These 8 phrases cover 90% of auto interactions.
constraint_note: Practice the full sequence: destination + price + directions. Say each phrase aloud 3 times.
example: Majestic-ge hogbeku. Eshtu aagutte? = I need to go to Majestic. How much? Meter haaki = Put the meter.
narrator_action: Learn: ___ ge hogbeku (I need to go to ___), Eshtu aagutte? (How much will it be?), Meter haaki (Put the meter), Tumba doora (Too far), Illi nilsi (Stop here), Bala tiru (Turn right), Edakke tiru (Turn left), Nidhaanavaagi hogi (Go slowly).

action: Shadow food ordering phrases. Match the rhythm of a darshini order.
description: Shadowing for ordering: Enu beku, Eradu idli kodi, Ondu kaapi kodi, Eshtu, Dhanyavada tumba chennagi.
context: Ordering at a darshini is fast — the server doesn't wait. Shadowing trains you to produce these phrases at natural speed.
constraint_note: Match the rhythm. Darshini orders flow as one phrase, not word by word.
example: [Slow] 'E-ra-du id-li ko-di' → [Fast] 'Eradidli kodi'
narrator_action: Shadow food ordering phrases. Match the rhythm of a darshini order.

description: Practice ordering food in Kannada. Role-play a restaurant interaction using only Kannada phrases.
action: Practice this sequence: Namaskara, menu kodi (give menu), Ondu masala dosa kodi (give one masala dosa), Ondu kaapi kodi (one coffee), Bill eshtu? (how much is the bill?), Dhanyavada.
context: Restaurant ordering is a safe, low-stakes way to practice Kannada. The interaction is predictable, so you can prepare and feel confident.
constraint_note: Say the full sequence aloud as if you're in a restaurant. Practice 3 times. Try varying the order each time.
example: ನಮಸ್ಕಾರ, ಒಂದು ಮಸಾಲೆ ದೋಸೆ ಕೊಡಿ, ಒಂದು ಕಾಫಿ ಕೊಡಿ. = Hello, give one masala dosa, give one coffee.

lines.speaker: You
lines.text: Ondu masala dosa, ondu kaapi kodi

narrator_action: Practice this sequence: Namaskara, menu kodi (give menu), Ondu masala dosa kodi (give one masala dosa), Ondu kaapi kodi (one coffee), Bill eshtu? (how much is the bill?), Dhanyavada.

action: Learn emergency and help phrases: Sahaya maadi (help me), Doctor yelli? (where's a doctor?), Police call maadi (call police), Nanage artha aagalla (I don't understand), Kannada baralla (I don't know Kannada), Nidhaanavaagi heli (say slowly).
description: Learn 6 emergency and help phrases. These are safety vocabulary — know them even if you never use them.
context: Emergency phrases should be automatic — you shouldn't have to think when you need help. Practice until they're reflexive.
constraint_note: Say each phrase 5 times. These should be memorized, not just recognized. Practice 'Nanage artha aagalla' — you'll use it often.
example: ಸಹಾಯ ಮಾಡಿ (Sahaya maadi)=Help me, ನನಗೆ ಅರ್ಥ ಆಗಲ್ಲ (Nanage artha aagalla)=I don't understand.

action: Practice introducing yourself in Kannada: Nanna hesaru ___ (My name is ___), Naanu ___-inda bandiddene (I came from ___), Naanu ___-alli kelsa maaduttene (I work at ___), Nanage Kannada kaliyoke ishta (I like learning Kannada).
description: Practice a full self-introduction in Kannada. This is the most common social interaction you'll have.
context: A Kannada self-introduction immediately changes how people perceive you. Even a basic attempt earns warmth and patience from native speakers.
constraint_note: Fill in your details. Say the full introduction aloud 3 times. Practice until it flows without pausing.
example: ನನ್ನ ಹೆಸರು Abhishek. ನಾನು Delhi-inda bandiddene. ನಾನು Bangalore-alli kelsa maaduttene. ನನಗೆ ಕನ್ನಡ ಕಲಿಯೋಕೆ ಇಷ್ಟ.

lines.speaker: You
lines.text: Eradu varsha aaytu. Nanage Kannada kaliyoke ishta

lines.speaker: Them
lines.text: Tumba chennagide! Kannada swalpa baratte alla?

narrator_action: Practice introducing yourself in Kannada: Nanna hesaru ___ (My name is ___), Naanu ___-inda bandiddene (I came from ___), Naanu ___-alli kelsa maaduttene (I work at ___), Nanage Kannada kaliyoke ishta (I like learning Kannada).

action: Role-play a complete auto ride in Kannada. Say aloud: greeting, destination, price negotiation, directions during the ride, asking to stop, paying, thanking.
description: Practice a full auto-rickshaw interaction from start to finish. This combines greetings, numbers, directions, and polite phrases.
context: The auto ride is the ultimate Kannada practice scenario — it's real, it's daily, and it uses vocabulary from multiple categories.
constraint_note: Say the entire interaction aloud. Include: greeting, where you want to go, asking the price, giving one direction, asking to stop, paying, saying thanks.
example: Namaskara. Koramangala-ge hogbeku. Eshtu? Tumba jaasti, swalpa kammi maadi. Sari. Illi bala tiru. Illi nilsi. Eshtu aaytu? Dhanyavada.

action: Practice a doctor visit conversation: 'Nanage hushaar illa' (I'm not well), 'Tala novu' (headache), 'Hotte novu' (stomach ache), 'Jvara ide' (I have fever), 'Yavaga shuru aaytu?' (When did it start?), 'Aushadhi kodi' (Give medicine).
description: Learn medical/health phrases for a doctor visit. These are important safety vocabulary.
context: Health phrases are critical — you may need them when you're least able to think clearly. Practice until they're automatic.
constraint_note: Role-play a full doctor visit aloud: greeting, describing symptoms, answering questions, asking about medicine.
example: ನನಗೆ ಹುಷಾರಿಲ್ಲ (Nanage hushaar illa)=I'm not well. ತಲೆ ನೋವು (Tala novu)=Headache. ಜ್ವರ ಇದೆ (Jvara ide)=I have fever.

lines.speaker: Doctor
lines.text: Sari, aushadhi bareytene. Mooru dina rest maadi

narrator_action: Practice a doctor visit conversation: 'Nanage hushaar illa' (I'm not well), 'Tala novu' (headache), 'Hotte novu' (stomach ache), 'Jvara ide' (I have fever), 'Yavaga shuru aaytu?' (When did it start?), 'Aushadhi kodi' (Give medicine).

action: From memory, write out a complete auto ride conversation in Kannada. Include: greeting, destination, price, one direction, stop, payment, thanks.
description: Test yourself: write a full auto ride interaction from memory. This tests whether your phrase knowledge is usable.
context: If you can write it from memory, you can say it in real life. The auto ride is your benchmark scenario.
constraint_note: No peeking. Write the full conversation. Then check against your notes. Practice the parts you forgot.
example: Start: Namaskara. ___-ge hogbeku. Eshtu? ... End: Illi nilsi. Eshtu aaytu? Dhanyavada. Fill in the middle.

action: Shadow question phrases. Practice asking questions with natural intonation.
description: Shadowing for questions: Enu beku, Yelli, Eshtu, Yavaga, Kannada gottha.
context: Questions have rising intonation in Kannada. Shadowing helps you get the melody right — not just the words.
constraint_note: Focus on the rising tone at the end of each question.
example: [Slow] 'Kan-na-da got-thaa?' → [Fast] 'Kannada gottha?'
narrator_action: Shadow question phrases. Practice asking questions with natural intonation.

action: Learn 8 polite request phrases: Dayavittu (please), Kshamisi (excuse me/sorry), Swalpa kaali (wait a moment), Innomme heli (say again), Bareyiri (please write it), Torsiri (please show), Olage barobahudu? (may I come in?), Hogabahudu? (may I go?).
description: Learn polite request phrases. Politeness in Kannada is expressed through specific verb forms and words like 'dayavittu.'
context: Polite Kannada opens doors. 'Dayavittu swalpa nidhaanavaagi heli' (please say slowly) is a phrase you'll use constantly as a learner.
constraint_note: Practice each phrase 3 times. These should become reflexive — politeness should be automatic, not translated.
example: ದಯವಿಟ್ಟು (Dayavittu)=please, ಕ್ಷಮಿಸಿ (Kshamisi)=sorry/excuse me. 'Dayavittu innomme heli' = Please say that again.

action: Practice a full restaurant interaction: entering, asking for a table, ordering food and drinks, asking for the bill, paying, and leaving. All in Kannada.
description: Role-play a complete restaurant visit in Kannada. This combines food vocabulary, numbers, polite phrases, and basic conversation.
context: Restaurants are safe practice zones — the interaction is predictable, staff are usually patient, and you get immediate feedback.
constraint_note: Say the full interaction aloud. Include: greeting, seating, ordering 2 items, asking about something on the menu, getting the bill.
example: Namaskara. Eradu janakke jagah ide? Ondu masala dosa, ondu idli. Kaapi bisi aagirli. Bill kodi. Eshtu aaytu? Dhanyavada.

action: Learn phone conversation phrases: 'Hello, yaaru maatadtiddiru?' (Who's speaking?), 'Swalpa kaali' (Hold on), '___ iddara?' (Is ___ there?), 'Naanu ___ maatadtiddene' (This is ___), 'Nantara phone maadi' (Call later), 'Message maadi' (Send a message).
description: Learn 6 phone conversation phrases. Phone calls in Kannada are common — delivery people, building staff, local services.
context: Phone Kannada is harder than face-to-face because you can't use gestures. Having set phrases ready makes phone calls manageable.
constraint_note: Practice a phone call: answer, identify yourself, ask for someone, say you'll call back. Say aloud.
example: Hello, yaaru maatadtiddiru? Naanu Abhishek maatadtiddene. ___ iddara? Swalpa kaali. Nantara phone maadi.

action: Shadow number and price phrases. Practice saying amounts fluently.
description: Shadowing for numbers and prices: Ondu-Aidu, Hattu rupaayi, Mooru nooru rupaayi, Ottu eshtu aayitu.
context: Numbers need to be automatic — you can't pause to think when someone tells you a price. Shadow until counting feels natural.
constraint_note: Count smoothly without pausing between numbers.
example: [Slow] 'On-du, E-ra-du, Moo-ru' → [Fast] 'Ondu eradu mooru'
narrator_action: Shadow number and price phrases. Practice saying amounts fluently.

action: Practice asking about someone's family. Speak the visitor's lines.
description: Dialogue: Ramu visits Lakshmi and they talk about family.
context: Listen first, then speak Ramu's lines.
constraint_note: Focus on Ramu's lines.
example: Lakshmi: Nimma amma hegiddare? → You: Amma chennagiddare, dhanyavada.

lines.speaker: Lakshmi
lines.text: Nanna maga Mysuru-alli kelsa maadthane. Magalu illi ide.
lines.audio_text: ನನ್ನ ಮಗ ಮೈಸೂರಿನಲ್ಲಿ ಕೆಲಸ ಮಾಡ್ತಾನೆ. ಮಗಳು ಇಲ್ಲಿ ಇದೆ.

lines.speaker: Ramu
lines.text: Tumba chennagi. Nanna akka kuda naale barthale.
lines.audio_text: ತುಂಬಾ ಚೆನ್ನಾಗಿ. ನನ್ನ ಅಕ್ಕ ಕೂಡ ನಾಳೆ ಬರ್ತಾಳೆ.

lines.speaker: Lakshmi
lines.text: Tumba khushi! Avaru kuda chai-ge banni!
lines.audio_text: ತುಂಬಾ ಖುಷಿ! ಅವರು ಕೂಡ ಚಾಯ್‌ಗೆ ಬನ್ನಿ!

narrator_action: Practice asking about someone's family. Speak the visitor's lines.

constraint_note: Say it before the answer plays.
example: 'Ask how much this costs' → 'Idara bele eshtu?'

Say: too expensive!
Say: give for thirty rupees.
Say: give one kilo tomatoes.
Ask: is there another color?
Ask: what's the total?
Say: okay, give it.
Full shopping: ask price, bargain, ask total, pay.
prompts.answer_audio: ಇದರ ಬೆಲೆ ಎಷ್ಟು? ತುಂಬಾ ಜಾಸ್ತಿ! ಸ್ವಲ್ಪ ಕಮ್ಮಿ ಮಾಡಿ. ಒಟ್ಟು ಎಷ್ಟು? ಸರಿ, ಧನ್ಯವಾದ.

narrator_action: Production drill: shopping and bargaining. Hear English, say Kannada.

action: Learn 6 opinion phrases: 'Nanage ishta' (I like it), 'Nanage ishta illa' (I don't like it), 'Chennagide' (It's good), 'Tumba chennagide' (Very good), 'Sari illa' (Not okay), 'Nanage gothilla' (I don't know).
description: Learn to express opinions and preferences. These move you from transactional to conversational Kannada.
context: Expressing opinions is where personality enters language. 'Nanage ee cinema ishta' (I like this movie) is a real conversation, not a transaction.
constraint_note: Express 5 real opinions about things you like/dislike using these phrases. Say aloud.
example: ನನಗೆ ಇಷ್ಟ (Nanage ishta)=I like it. 'Nanage filter kaapi tumba ishta' = I really like filter coffee.

action: Practice giving directions to your home in Kannada. Start from a landmark: '___ inda nere hogi, ___ hattira bala tiru, ___ nantara edakke tiru, ___ mane.'
description: Give directions to your home in Kannada. This combines place words, direction words, and landmarks into a practical skill.
context: Giving directions is a real test of spatial vocabulary. If you can direct an auto to your home in Kannada, your navigation vocabulary is solid.
constraint_note: Start from a landmark near your home. Use: nere hogi (go straight), bala/edakke tiru (turn right/left), hattira (near), nantara (after).
example: Silk Board-inda nere hogi, signal hattira bala tiru, Big Bazaar nantara edakke tiru, eradu nimisha nantara bala kade nanna mane.

action: Learn 6 time-telling phrases: 'Eega eshtu gante?' (What time is it now?), 'Mooru gante' (3 o'clock), 'Mooru-a-vare' (3:30), 'Aidu nimisha' (5 minutes), 'Arda gante' (half hour), 'Hatthu nimisha-dalli baruttene' (I'll come in 10 minutes).
description: Learn to tell and ask about time in Kannada. Time phrases are essential for making plans and appointments.
context: Time-telling combines numbers with specific time vocabulary. 'Hatthu nimisha-dalli baruttene' (I'll come in 10 minutes) is something you'll say often.
constraint_note: Practice telling the current time in Kannada. Then make 3 time-based plans: 'Naale beligge hattu gantege sigona' (Shall we meet tomorrow morning at 10?).
example: ಈಗ ಎಷ್ಟು ಗಂಟೆ? (Eega eshtu gante?)=What time is it? ಮೂರು ಗಂಟೆ (Mooru gante)=3 o'clock.

action: Shadow family introduction phrases. Practice talking about your family.
description: Shadowing for family: Nanna hesaru, Nanna amma Delhi-alli, Nanna akka naale barthale, Nanna kutumba dodda.
context: Family introductions come up in every social interaction in Karnataka. These phrases need to flow naturally.
constraint_note: Shadow the full sentences, not just individual words.
example: [Slow] 'Nan-na a-mma Del-hi-yal-li id-daa-re' → [Fast] natural flow
narrator_action: Shadow family introduction phrases. Practice talking about your family.

narrator_action: Practice visiting a temple. Speak the visitor's lines.

action: From memory, write all the polite phrases you know: please, sorry, thank you, excuse me, wait, say again, may I. Check yourself.
description: Recall all polite/courtesy phrases from memory. Politeness should be automatic — test if it is.
context: Polite phrases need to be reflexive. If you have to think about how to say 'sorry' or 'please,' it's not automatic enough yet.
constraint_note: Write every polite phrase you remember. Then check. The ones you forgot need more practice.
example: Dayavittu, Kshamisi, Dhanyavada, Swalpa kaali, Innomme heli... How many can you recall?

action: Practice a landlord/maintenance conversation: 'Neeru baruttilla' (water isn't coming), 'Light hogide' (power is out), 'Plumber kalisiri' (send a plumber), 'Yaavaga sari aagutte?' (When will it be fixed?), 'Rent kottiddene' (I've paid rent).
description: Learn housing/maintenance phrases. If you live in Bangalore, you'll need these with your landlord, watchman, or maintenance staff.
context: Housing interactions are often in Kannada even if your work is in English. These phrases handle 90% of maintenance conversations.
constraint_note: Role-play a complaint call: describe the problem, ask for help, ask when it'll be fixed. Say aloud.
example: ನೀರು ಬರುತ್ತಿಲ್ಲ (Neeru baruttilla)=Water isn't coming. ಯಾವಾಗ ಸರಿ ಆಗುತ್ತೆ? (Yaavaga sari aagutte?)=When will it be fixed?

action: Practice small talk: 'Yelli kelsa?' (Where do you work?), 'Eshtu varsha aaytu Bangalore-alli?' (How many years in Bangalore?), 'Moola yelli?' (Where are you originally from?), 'Weekend enu plan?' (What's the weekend plan?).
description: Learn small talk phrases. These are the conversations that happen with colleagues, neighbours, and acquaintances.
context: Small talk in Kannada transforms relationships. Your watchman, your colleague, your chai wallah — a few phrases in their language changes everything.
constraint_note: Practice a 2-minute small talk conversation with an imaginary colleague. Include: greeting, asking about work, weekend plans, goodbye.
example: Hegiddira? Kelsa hege nadeyuttide? Weekend enu plan? Sari, nodona. Hogi banni! — A complete small talk exchange.

action: Shadow time and planning phrases. Practice making plans in Kannada.
description: Shadowing for time: Ivattu beligge, Naale saanje, Naale beligge barthini, Mooru ghante aagutte.
context: Making plans requires smooth time expressions. These are the phrases you'll use when coordinating with people.
constraint_note: Time expressions should flow as one unit — 'naale beligge' not 'naale... beligge'.
example: [Slow] 'Naa-le be-lig-ge bar-thee-ni' → [Fast] natural flow
narrator_action: Shadow time and planning phrases. Practice making plans in Kannada.

action: Practice a phone call to order food delivery: 'Namaskara, order maadabeku' → items → address → 'Eshtu time aagutte?' → 'Dhanyavada.'
description: Role-play ordering food delivery in Kannada. A practical scenario you can actually use.
context: Food delivery calls are a safe, real practice opportunity. The interaction is predictable and the stakes are low.
constraint_note: Say the full call aloud: greeting, order 2 items, give your address, ask delivery time, thank them.
example: Namaskara, ondu biryani mattu ondu gobi manchurian. Address: ___. Eshtu time aagutte? Sari, dhanyavada.

action: Practice asking for and understanding directions: 'Majestic-ge hege hogbeku?' Then understand: 'Nere hogi, signal-alli bala tiru, 500 meter nantara edakke ide.'
description: Practice both giving AND receiving directions. Understanding spoken directions is harder than giving them.
context: Receiving directions tests listening comprehension. In real life, people speak fast — practice parsing direction sequences.
constraint_note: Write directions to 3 places you know. Then read them aloud as if someone asked you. Practice both roles.
example: Q: 'MG Road-ge hege hogbeku?' A: 'Illi-inda nere hogi, eradu signal nantara bala tiru, metro station hattira ide.'

action: Write a complete self-introduction in Kannada from memory: name, origin, work, how long in Bangalore, what you like about Karnataka, one hobby. 6+ sentences.
description: Full self-introduction from memory. This is your Kannada elevator pitch — it should flow naturally.
context: If you can introduce yourself fluently in Kannada, you've crossed a major threshold. This is the single most useful thing to have memorized.
constraint_note: No peeking. Write it out. Then say it aloud 3 times. Time yourself — aim for under 30 seconds.
example: Namaskara. Nanna hesaru ___. Naanu ___-inda. ___ varsha-inda Bangalore-alli iddene. Nanage Karnataka tumba ishta. Nanage ___ ishta.

action: Shadow shopping phrases. Practice bargaining at market speed.
description: Shadowing for shopping: Idara bele eshtu, Tumba jaasti, Swalpa kammi maadi, Bere banna idya, Sari kodi.
context: Market bargaining is fast-paced. You need these phrases ready without thinking.
constraint_note: Bargaining phrases should sound firm, not tentative.
example: [Slow] 'Tum-ba jaas-ti' → [Fast] 'Tumba jaasti!'
narrator_action: Shadow shopping phrases. Practice bargaining at market speed.
