action: Practice a complete shopping interaction at a clothing store: browsing, asking size/color, trying on, negotiating price, paying. All in Kannada.
description: Role-play a clothing shop visit. This combines vocabulary (clothes, colors, numbers) with phrases (asking, negotiating, paying).
context: Clothing shopping is a longer interaction than buying vegetables — it tests sustained Kannada conversation over multiple exchanges.
constraint_note: Full interaction aloud: enter, browse, ask about an item, ask for different size/color, ask price, negotiate, decide, pay.
example: Ee shirt bere size-alli ide? Large beku. Bere banna? Neeli ide? Eshtu? Swalpa kammi maadi. Sari, card swipe maadbahudu?

action: Learn 6 agreement/disagreement phrases: 'Sari' (okay/right), 'Haudu' (yes), 'Illa' (no), 'Nija' (true), 'Sari alla' (not right), 'Nanage ondu abhipraaya ide' (I have an opinion).
description: Learn to agree and disagree in Kannada. These are conversational essentials.
context: Agreement/disagreement phrases are the backbone of conversation. 'Haudu, nija' (yes, true) or 'Illa, sari alla' (no, that's not right) — you need these constantly.
constraint_note: Practice responding to imaginary statements with agreement or disagreement. Vary your responses.
example: ಹೌದು (Haudu)=yes, ಇಲ್ಲ (Illa)=no, ಸರಿ (Sari)=okay/correct, ನಿಜ (Nija)=true. 'Haudu, nija heltiddira' = Yes, you're saying the truth.

action: Practice a complete bank/post office interaction: 'Nanage account open maadabeku' (I need to open an account), 'ID proof ide' (I have ID proof), 'Form kodi' (give the form), 'Sign yelli maadabeku?' (Where to sign?).
description: Learn formal/official interaction phrases. Banks, post offices, and government offices often operate in Kannada.
context: Official interactions in Karnataka often default to Kannada. Having basic phrases ready prevents the awkward 'English please' moment.
constraint_note: Role-play a bank visit: enter, state purpose, provide documents, ask questions, thank. Say aloud.
example: Namaskara. Nanage savings account open maadabeku. ID proof — Aadhaar card ide. Form kodi. Sign yelli? Dhanyavada.

action: Shadow direction phrases. Practice giving directions confidently.
description: Shadowing for directions: Nere hogi, Bala tiru, Edakke tiru, Mundhe hogi, Hattira ide.
context: When someone asks you for directions, you need to respond quickly. These phrases should be automatic.
constraint_note: Direction commands are short and sharp. Shadow them with confidence.
example: [Slow] 'Ne-re ho-gi, ba-la ti-ru' → [Fast] 'Nere hogi, bala tiru'
narrator_action: Shadow direction phrases. Practice giving directions confidently.

action: Practice a conversation with your building watchman/security: greeting, asking about packages, reporting a visitor, asking about water/power schedule. All in Kannada.
description: Role-play a watchman interaction. This is one of the most common daily Kannada conversations for apartment dwellers in Bangalore.
context: Your building watchman likely speaks Kannada as a first language. Even basic Kannada with them builds a real relationship.
constraint_note: Full interaction: greeting, ask about a delivery, tell them a friend is coming, ask about water timing. Say aloud.
example: Namaskara. Ivattu yaavadu parcel bandide? Nanna snehita baruttane, olage bidiri. Neeru yaavaga baratte? Dhanyavada.

action: Read this short story and learn the highlighted Kannada words in context.
description: A short story about Ramu's morning at a chai stall, with Kannada vocabulary embedded.
context: Learning words in stories is 3x more effective than learning from lists. The narrative gives each word a memory anchor.
constraint_note: Read the story first, then test yourself on the vocabulary.
example: After reading, you should know: angadi, kaapi, bisi, sakkare, haal.

segments.text: Ramu woke up early and walked to the angadi — the small shop near his mane, his house.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu woke up early and walked to the small shop near his house.

segments.text: He said: Namaskara! Ondu bisi kaapi kodi — one hot coffee please.
segments.narrator_before: He greeted the shopkeeper and asked for one hot coffee.

segments.text: The shopkeeper asked: Sakkare beku? Do you want sugar?
segments.narrator_before: The shopkeeper asked: do you want sugar?

segments.text: Ramu said: Haudu, swalpa sakkare, mattu swalpa haal — yes, a little sugar, and a little milk.
segments.narrator_before: Ramu said: yes, a little sugar, and a little milk.

segments.text: He sat on a kurchi — a chair — and watched the beligge — the morning — wake up around him.
segments.narrator_before: He sat on a chair and watched the morning wake up around him.

segments.text: The kaapi was tumba chennagide — very good. Ramu smiled and said: Dhanyavada!
segments.narrator_before: The coffee was very good. Ramu smiled and said thank you.

questions.prompt: What did Ramu ask for in his coffee?
questions.narrator_before: What did Ramu ask for in his coffee?
questions.narrator_after: The answer is: sugar () and milk ()

narrator_action: Read this short story and learn the highlighted Kannada words in context.

action: Follow Priya's auto ride and learn the phrases she uses.
description: A story about Priya taking an auto-rickshaw, with common travel phrases embedded.
context: Auto ride phrases in a story context are easier to remember than a phrase list.
constraint_note: Pay attention to what Priya says at each step of the ride.
example: After reading, you should be able to handle a basic auto conversation.

segments.text: Priya stood at the road and waved at an auto. The driver stopped.
segments.narrator_before: Priya stood at the road and waved at an auto. The driver stopped.

segments.text: She said: Namaskara, Majestic-ge hogbeku — I need to go to Majestic.
segments.narrator_before: She greeted him and said she needed to go to Majestic.

segments.text: The driver said: Eshtu doora — how far is it? She replied: Hattira, 3 kilometer — it's nearby, 3 kilometers.
segments.narrator_before: The driver asked how far it was. She replied: it's nearby, about 3 kilometers.

segments.text: He said: Nooru rupaayi — 100 rupees. She said: Tumba jaasti! Aivattu kodi — too much! Give 50.
segments.narrator_before: He said: 100 rupees. She said: that's too much! Give it for 50.

segments.text: They agreed on eppattu — 70 rupees. She got in and said: Nere hogi — go straight.
segments.narrator_before: They agreed on 70 rupees. She got in and said: go straight.

segments.text: Near Majestic she said: Illi nilsi — stop here. She paid and said: Dhanyavada — thank you.
segments.narrator_before: Near Majestic she said: stop here. She paid and said thank you.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'I need to go to' in Kannada?
questions.narrator_before: How do you say 'I need to go to' in Kannada?
questions.narrator_after: The answer is: I need to go to a place.
The answer is: too much / too expensive

constraint_note: Try to shadow the entire flow without stopping. It's okay to stumble — keep going.
example: Full flow: Namaskara → order → eshtu → pay → dhanyavada → hogi barthini

action: Read about Meera's visit to the market and learn food and shopping vocabulary.
description: A story about shopping at a local market with food vocabulary and numbers embedded.
context: Market vocabulary in a story sticks better because you can picture the scene.
constraint_note: Notice the food words and numbers used in the story.
example: After reading, you should know: hannu, tarkari, eerulli, tomato, aalu, and numbers in context.

segments.text: Meera went to the pete — the market — on Saturday beligge — morning.
segments.narrator_before: Meera went to the market on Saturday morning.

segments.text: First she bought tarkari — vegetables. Eradu kg aalu — 2 kilos of potatoes — and ondu kg eerulli — 1 kilo of onions.
segments.narrator_before: First she bought vegetables. 2 kilos of potatoes and 1 kilo of onions.

segments.text: The tarkari-wallah said: Tomato beku? Tumba fresh! She said: Haudu, ardhaa kg kodi — yes, give half a kilo.
segments.narrator_before: The vegetable seller asked: want tomatoes? They're very fresh! She said: yes, give me half a kilo.

segments.text: Then she went to the hannu angadi — the fruit shop. She bought mooru baalehannu — 3 bananas — and eradu maavinhannu — 2 mangoes.
segments.narrator_before: Then she went to the fruit shop. She bought 3 bananas and 2 mangoes.

segments.text: Bele eshtu? — How much total? The shopkeeper said: Nooru ippattu rupaayi — 120 rupees.
segments.narrator_before: How much total? The shopkeeper said: 120 rupees.

segments.text: Meera said: Sari — okay, and paid. She carried her cheela — bag — home, happy with her fresh food.
segments.narrator_before: Meera said okay and paid. She carried her bag home, happy with her fresh food.

questions.prompt: How many kilos of potatoes did Meera buy?
questions.narrator_before: How many kilos of potatoes did Meera buy?

questions.prompt: How much was the total bill?
questions.narrator_before: How much was the total bill?

action: Follow Kiran's day and notice how Kannada verbs change with tense.
description: A story about Kiran's daily routine that demonstrates present, past, and future tense naturally.
context: Grammar in stories is absorbed naturally — you hear the patterns without memorizing rules.
constraint_note: Notice the verb endings: -uttene (present), -de (past), -uttene with naale (future).
example: After reading, you should recognize present, past, and future tense patterns.

segments.text: Kiran's day started early. Ninne — yesterday — he got up at aaru gante — 6 o'clock. Avanu beligge eddha — he woke up in the morning.
segments.narrator_before: Kiran's day started early. Yesterday he got up at 6 o'clock. He woke up in the morning.

segments.text: Ivattu — today — he is busy. Avanu kelsa maaduttiddane — he is working. Avanu office-alli iddane — he is at the office.
segments.narrator_before: Today he is busy. He is working. He is at the office.

segments.text: At madhyahna — afternoon — avanu oota maadda — he ate lunch. Anna mattu saaru — rice and rasam.
segments.narrator_before: In the afternoon, he ate lunch. Rice and rasam.

segments.text: Saanje — in the evening — avanu manege baruttane — he comes home. Avanu TV noduttane — he watches TV.
segments.narrator_before: In the evening, he comes home. He watches TV.

segments.text: Naale — tomorrow — avanu Mysore-ge hoguttane — he will go to Mysore. Avanu snehitanige siguttane — he will meet his friend.
segments.narrator_before: Tomorrow he will go to Mysore. He will meet his friend.

segments.text: Kiran is happy. Ivattu kelsa, naale prayana — today work, tomorrow travel!
segments.narrator_before: Kiran is happy. Today is for work, tomorrow is for travel!

questions.prompt: What verb ending indicates past tense? (he woke up = eddha)
questions.narrator_before: What verb ending indicates past tense? (he woke up = eddha)
What tense does this verb form indicate?
questions.narrator_after: The answer is: He is doing (present continuous)

questions.prompt: How do you know 'hoguttane' is future?
questions.narrator_before: How do you know this verb is future tense?
questions.narrator_after: The answer is: it's used with tomorrow — future context.

narrator_action: Follow Kiran's day and notice how Kannada verbs change with tense.

action: Read about Ananya's experience at a Karnataka home and learn about hospitality culture.
description: A story about visiting a Kannada family, demonstrating hospitality phrases and customs.
context: Cultural context makes phrases meaningful — you learn not just the words but when and why to use them.
constraint_note: Notice the hospitality sequence: invite, seat, offer drink, offer food.
example: After reading, you should understand Karnataka hospitality norms and key phrases.

segments.text: Ananya visited her colleague Lakshmi's mane — house — for the first time.
segments.narrator_before: Ananya visited her colleague Lakshmi's house for the first time.

segments.text: Lakshmi opened the baagilu — door — and said: Banni, banni! Olage banni! — Come, come! Come inside!
segments.narrator_before: Lakshmi opened the door and said: come in, come in! Come inside!

segments.text: She said: Kootko — please sit. Then immediately: Kaapi beku? Chai beku? — Want coffee? Want tea?
segments.narrator_before: She said: please sit. Then immediately: want coffee? Want tea?

segments.text: Ananya said: Kaapi, dayavittu — coffee, please. Lakshmi brought bisi kaapi — hot coffee — and khaara mixture — spicy snacks.
segments.narrator_before: Ananya said: coffee, please. Lakshmi brought hot coffee and spicy snacks.

segments.text: Then Lakshmi asked: Oota aaytu? — Have you eaten? This is not really a question — it's an invitation to eat.
segments.narrator_before: Then Lakshmi asked: have you eaten? This is not really a question — it's an invitation to eat.

segments.text: Ananya learned: in Karnataka, you never let a guest leave without offering food. Athithi devo bhava — the guest is God.
segments.narrator_before: Ananya learned: in Karnataka, you never let a guest leave without offering food. The guest is treated like God.

questions.narrator_after: The answer is: Come, come inside

questions.answer: It's an invitation to eat (not just asking if you've eaten)
questions.narrator_before: What does this phrase really mean?
questions.narrator_after: The answer is: It's an invitation to eat (not just asking if you've eaten)
The answer is: The guest is God

questions.answer: Invite in → seat → offer drink → offer food
questions.narrator_after: The answer is: Invite in → seat → offer drink → offer food

narrator_action: Read about Ananya's experience at a Karnataka home and learn about hospitality culture.

action: Follow Suresh at the doctor's office and learn medical phrases.
description: A story about a doctor visit with health-related phrases embedded.
context: Medical phrases are critical safety vocabulary. A story makes them memorable.
constraint_note: Pay attention to how Suresh describes his symptoms and the doctor's responses.

segments.text: Suresh was not feeling well. Nanage hushaar illa — I'm not well, he told his wife.
segments.narrator_before: Suresh was not feeling well. He told his wife: I'm not well.

segments.text: He went to the aaspathre — hospital. He told the doctor: Doctor, nanage tala novu — I have a headache.
segments.narrator_before: He went to the hospital. He told the doctor: Doctor, I have a headache.

segments.text: The doctor asked: Yaavaga shuru aaytu? — When did it start? Suresh said: Ninne raatri-inda — since last night.
segments.narrator_before: The doctor asked: when did it start? Suresh said: since last night.

segments.text: The doctor asked: Jvara ide? — Do you have fever? Suresh said: Haudu, swalpa jvara ide — yes, a little fever.
segments.narrator_before: The doctor asked: do you have fever? Suresh said: yes, a little fever.

segments.text: The doctor said: Chinta maadabeda — don't worry. Aushadhi bareytene — I'll write medicine. Mooru dina rest maadi — rest for 3 days.
segments.narrator_before: The doctor said: don't worry. I'll write you some medicine. Rest for 3 days.

segments.text: Suresh said: Dhanyavada, doctor. He took the aushadhi and went home to rest.
segments.narrator_before: Suresh said thank you, doctor. He took the medicine and went home to rest.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'I'm not well' in Kannada?
questions.narrator_before: How do you say 'I'm not well' in Kannada?
questions.narrator_after: The answer is: I'm not well.

narrator_action: Follow Suresh at the doctor's office and learn medical phrases.

action: Read about Divya's rainy day and learn weather and emotion words.
description: A story about a rainy day in Bangalore with weather and emotion vocabulary.
context: Weather and emotions are everyday conversation topics. A story connects them naturally.
constraint_note: Notice the weather words and how Divya's feelings change through the story.

segments.text: It was a mazhe dina — a rainy day — in Bengalooru. Divya looked out the kitiki — window.
segments.narrator_before: It was a rainy day in Bangalore. Divya looked out the window.

segments.text: The aakasha — sky — was kappu — black with mooda — clouds. Gaali was blowing — the wind was strong.
segments.narrator_before: The sky was dark with clouds. The wind was blowing strong.

segments.text: Divya felt swalpa bejaar — a little sad. She wanted bisilu — sunshine.
segments.narrator_before: Divya felt a little sad. She wanted sunshine.

segments.text: Then she made herself bisi kaapi — hot coffee. She sat near the kitiki and listened to the mazhe — rain.
segments.narrator_before: Then she made herself hot coffee. She sat near the window and listened to the rain.

segments.text: Slowly she felt shanta — peaceful. The rain was sundara — beautiful. She felt khushi — happy.
segments.narrator_before: Slowly she felt peaceful. The rain was beautiful. She felt happy.

segments.text: Sometimes mazhe brings the best moments, she thought.
segments.narrator_before: Sometimes rain brings the best moments, she thought.

questions.prompt: What two emotions did Divya feel?
questions.narrator_before: What two emotions did Divya feel?

narrator_action: Read about Divya's rainy day and learn weather and emotion words.

action: Follow Arjun as he gives directions to a lost tourist.
description: A story about giving directions, with direction and place vocabulary embedded.
context: Direction words in a real scenario are much easier to remember than in a list.
constraint_note: Trace the route in your mind as you read.

segments.text: A tourist stopped Arjun on MG Road. Kshamisi — excuse me, she said. Lalbagh yelli? — Where is Lalbagh?
segments.narrator_before: A tourist stopped Arjun on MG Road. Excuse me, she said. Where is Lalbagh?

segments.text: Arjun said: Illi-inda nere hogi — from here go straight. Eradu signal nantara — after 2 signals.
segments.narrator_before: Arjun said: from here, go straight. After 2 signals.

segments.text: Nantara bala tiru — then turn right. Ondu doddu raste baratte — a big road will come.
segments.narrator_before: Then turn right. A big road will come.

segments.text: Aa raste-alli edakke tiru — on that road turn left. Aidu nimisha nadeyiri — walk for 5 minutes.
segments.narrator_before: On that road, turn left. Walk for 5 minutes.

segments.text: Lalbagh gate nimage kanisatte — you will see the Lalbagh gate. Idu hattira — it's nearby, tumba doora alla — not very far.
segments.narrator_before: You will see the Lalbagh gate. It's nearby, not very far.

segments.text: The tourist said: Tumba dhanyavada! — Thank you very much! Arjun smiled: Banni, welcome!
segments.narrator_before: The tourist said: thank you very much! Arjun smiled and said: welcome!

narrator_action: Follow Arjun as he gives directions to a lost tourist.

action: Read about Kavya's weekend plans and notice how she talks about past, present, and future.
description: A story demonstrating all three tenses naturally through weekend planning.
context: Hearing all three tenses in one natural conversation helps you internalize the patterns.
constraint_note: Notice: past (-de/-da), present (-uttene/-uttane), future (naale + verb).

segments.text: Kavya called her friend Nisha. Nisha, ninne naanu tumba kelsa maadde — yesterday I worked a lot, she said.
segments.narrator_before: Kavya called her friend Nisha. Yesterday I worked a lot, she said.

segments.text: Ivattu naanu maneyalli iddene — today I am at home. Naanu rest maaduttiddene — I am resting.
segments.narrator_before: Today I am at home. I am resting.

segments.text: Nisha asked: Naale enu plan? — What's the plan for tomorrow?
segments.narrator_before: Nisha asked: what's the plan for tomorrow?

segments.text: Kavya said: Naale naanu Cubbon Park-ge hoguttene — tomorrow I will go to Cubbon Park.
segments.narrator_before: Kavya said: tomorrow I will go to Cubbon Park.

segments.text: Neenu baruttiya? — Will you come? Nisha said: Haudu! Naanu baruttene! — Yes! I will come!
segments.narrator_before: Will you come? Nisha said: yes! I will come!

segments.text: Kavya said: Chennagide! Beligge hattu gantege sigona — Great! Let's meet at 10 in the morning.
segments.narrator_before: Kavya said: great! Let's meet at 10 in the morning.

questions.prompt: Which word signals past tense in 'kelsa maadde'?
questions.narrator_before: Which word signals past tense in this phrase?
questions.narrator_after: The answer is: the ending signals past tense — I did.

questions.prompt: Which word signals present continuous in 'rest maaduttiddene'?
questions.narrator_before: Which word signals present continuous in this phrase?
questions.narrator_after: The answer is: the ending signals present continuous — I am doing.
The answer is: tomorrow plus will go — future tense.
The answer is: shall we meet
The answer is: will you come?

action: Read about Raj's first Ugadi celebration and learn about Karnataka festivals.
description: A story about experiencing Ugadi for the first time, with cultural vocabulary.
context: Festival stories teach culture and vocabulary together — the most natural way to learn.
constraint_note: Notice the festival-specific words and customs.

segments.text: Raj moved to Bengalooru from Delhi. His colleague invited him for Ugadi — the Kannada New Year.
segments.narrator_before: Raj moved to Bangalore from Delhi. His colleague invited him for the Kannada New Year celebration.

segments.text: At the mane — house — the baagilu — door — had a toran — a mango leaf decoration. Raj said: Tumba sundara! — Very beautiful!
segments.narrator_before: At the house, the door had a mango leaf decoration. Raj said: very beautiful!

segments.text: His colleague's amma — mother — gave him bevu-bella. Bevu is neem — bitter. Bella is jaggery — sweet.
segments.narrator_before: His colleague's mother gave him a mix of neem and jaggery. Neem is bitter. Jaggery is sweet.

segments.text: She said: Jeevana-dalli bevu-bella ide — life has bitter and sweet. We accept both. This is the meaning of Ugadi.
segments.narrator_before: She said: life has bitter and sweet. We accept both. This is the meaning of the new year.

segments.text: They ate holige — a sweet flatbread — and obbattu. Everyone said: Ugadi shubhashayagalu! — Happy Ugadi!
segments.narrator_before: They ate a sweet flatbread. Everyone wished each other a happy new year!

segments.text: Raj felt like he belonged. He said: Nanna hosa mane, nanna hosa habba — my new home, my new festival.
segments.narrator_before: Raj felt like he belonged. He said: this is my new home, my new festival.

questions.narrator_after: The answer is: Kannada New Year
The answer is: life's bitter and sweet moments — neem and jaggery.
The answer is: mango leaf decoration (on the door)

questions.narrator_before: How do you say happy new year?
questions.narrator_after: The answer is: the new year greeting!

narrator_action: Read about Raj's first Ugadi celebration and learn about Karnataka festivals.

description: Learn the first 5 Kannada vowels (ಅ ಆ ಇ ಈ ಉ). Write each one 5 times on paper. Say the sound out loud as you write.
action: Learn and write the first 5 Kannada vowels: ಅ ಆ ಇ ಈ ಉ. Write each one 5 times.
context: Kannada has 14 vowels. Starting with the first 5 builds the foundation — these are the most common sounds and the shapes your hand needs to learn first.
constraint_note: Write on paper, not a screen. Say the sound out loud as you write each letter. 5 repetitions per vowel.
example: ಅ = 'a' (as in 'about'), ಆ = 'aa' (as in 'father'), ಇ = 'i' (as in 'it'), ಈ = 'ee' (as in 'feet'), ಉ = 'u' (as in 'put').

description: Learn the next 5 Kannada vowels (ಊ ಋ ಎ ಏ ಐ). Write each one 5 times. Notice how the shapes relate to the first set.
action: Learn and write vowels 6-10: ಊ ಋ ಎ ಏ ಐ. Write each one 5 times.
context: Kannada vowels come in pairs (short/long). Noticing the pattern — ಉ/ಊ, ಎ/ಏ — helps you learn faster because the shapes are related.
constraint_note: Write on paper. Say each sound aloud. Notice which vowels look similar to each other.
example: ಊ = 'oo' (as in 'food'), ಎ = 'e' (as in 'bed'), ಏ = 'ay' (as in 'day'), ಐ = 'ai' (as in 'eye').

description: Learn the remaining Kannada vowels (ಒ ಓ ಔ ಅಂ ಅಃ). Write each 5 times. You now know all 14 vowels.
action: Learn and write the final 4 vowels + 2 modifiers: ಒ ಓ ಔ ಅಂ ಅಃ.
context: With these, you've completed all Kannada vowels. ಅಂ (anusvara) and ಅಃ (visarga) are special — they modify sounds rather than standing alone.
constraint_note: Write each 5 times. Say aloud. After finishing, try writing all 14 vowels from memory.
example: ಒ = 'o' (as in 'go'), ಓ = 'oh' (longer), ಔ = 'ou' (as in 'how'), ಅಂ = nasal 'm' sound, ಅಃ = breathy 'h' sound.

description: Learn the first row of Kannada consonants — the 'ka' group: ಕ ಖ ಗ ಘ ಙ. Write each 5 times and say the sound.
action: Learn the 'ka' group consonants: ಕ ಖ ಗ ಘ ಙ. Write each 5 times.
context: Kannada consonants are organized by where in the mouth the sound is made. The 'ka' group uses the back of the throat. This grouping helps you remember them.
constraint_note: Write on paper. Say each sound. Notice the pattern: unaspirated, aspirated, voiced, voiced-aspirated, nasal.
example: ಕ = 'ka', ಖ = 'kha', ಗ = 'ga', ಘ = 'gha', ಙ = 'nga'. If you know Hindi, the pattern is identical — ka, kha, ga, gha, nga.

description: Learn the 'cha' group consonants: ಚ ಛ ಜ ಝ ಞ. Write each 5 times. These are palatal sounds — made with the middle of the tongue.
action: Learn the 'cha' group: ಚ ಛ ಜ ಝ ಞ. Write each 5 times.
context: Second row of consonants — palatal sounds. Same pattern as the 'ka' group: unaspirated, aspirated, voiced, voiced-aspirated, nasal.
constraint_note: Write and say aloud. After finishing, try writing the 'ka' group from memory too — spaced repetition.
example: ಚ = 'cha', ಛ = 'chha', ಜ = 'ja', ಝ = 'jha', ಞ = 'nya'.

description: Learn numbers 1-10 in Kannada. Write the Kannada word and numeral for each. Say them aloud in sequence until you can do it without looking.
action: Learn Kannada numbers 1-10: Ondu, Eradu, Mooru, Nalku, Aidu, Aaru, Elu, Entu, Ombattu, Hattu.
context: Numbers are among the most useful vocabulary — you need them for prices, addresses, time, and phone numbers. Kannada numbers are used daily in markets and shops.
constraint_note: Say them in sequence until you can recite 1-10 without looking. Then try random recall: what's 7? What's 4?
example: ೧ ಒಂದು (Ondu)=1, ೨ ಎರಡು (Eradu)=2, ೩ ಮೂರು (Mooru)=3, ೪ ನಾಲ್ಕು (Nalku)=4, ೫ ಐದು (Aidu)=5.

details.label: Use
details.value: Ondu kaapi kodi = Give one coffee

items.body: Pronounced ON-du with stress on the first syllable. The 'on' sounds like 'fund' without the 'f'. This is the most common number you'll use — ordering 'ondu coffee' (one coffee) at a darshini (quick-service restaurant). Kannada numerals (೧) are used on bus route numbers and price tags, so recognizing them helps in daily life.

details.label: Use
details.value: Eradu idli kodi = Give two idlis

items.body: Pronounced EH-ra-du, three syllables. The 'r' is a soft tap, not the hard English 'r'. You'll hear this constantly in bargaining: 'eradu hundred' (two hundred). Memory aid: 'era' sounds like 'era' in English — think 'two eras of history.'

Pronounced MOO-ru, like 'moo' (cow sound) + 'ru'. The long 'oo' is important — 'muru' (short) means something different. Used in the common phrase 'mooru dina' (three days). Kannada loves the number three — many folk sayings come in threes.

Pronounced NAAL-ku. The 'aa' is long, like 'father'. The 'lk' combination is tricky for English speakers — practice saying 'silk' and isolating the 'lk' sound. Memory aid: 'NAAL' sounds like 'null' — four letters in 'null.'

Pronounced EYE-du, like the English word 'eye' + 'du'. One of the easiest numbers to remember because of the English connection. 'Aidu nimisha' (five minutes) is a phrase you'll hear constantly — it usually means 'sometime between 5 and 30 minutes.'
Pronounced AA-ru with a long 'aa' like 'father'. Don't confuse with 'aaru' meaning 'river' — same pronunciation, context makes it clear. Memory aid: 'AA-ru' has two syllables, and 6 is 2×3.

Pronounced AY-lu. The 'ḷ' is a retroflex 'l' — curl your tongue back and touch the roof of your mouth. This sound doesn't exist in English and is one of Kannada's distinctive features. Practice by saying 'lu' with your tongue curled back.

Pronounced EN-tu, like 'enter' without the 'er'. The nasal 'n' before 't' is natural for English speakers. Memory aid: 'eight' and 'entu' both start with 'e'. You'll hear 'entu ghante' (eight o'clock) for morning work start times.

Pronounced OM-bat-tu, three syllables. The double 't' is important — hold the 't' sound slightly longer. This is the hardest number to remember because it doesn't resemble any English word. Memory aid: 'OM-bat' — imagine a bat doing yoga (om) — that's nine lives of weirdness.

Pronounced HUT-tu (rhymes with 'put-tu'). The aspirated 'h' at the start is soft. Double 't' again — Kannada loves geminated consonants. 'Hattu rupaayi' (ten rupees) is the base unit for small purchases. Once you know 1-10, you can construct larger numbers: 'hattu-ondu' = 11, 'eradu-hattu' = 20.

narrator_action: Learn Kannada numbers 1-10: Ondu, Eradu, Mooru, Nalku, Aidu, Aaru, Elu, Entu, Ombattu, Hattu.

description: Learn 10 common food words in Kannada. These are words you'll encounter at restaurants and when ordering food.
action: Learn these food words: Anna (rice), Rotti (bread), Saaru (rasam), Huli (sambar), Mosaru (curd), Neeru (water), Kaapi (coffee), Chai (tea), Hannina Rasa (juice), Uppinakayi (pickle).
context: Food vocabulary is immediately useful — you can start using these words today at any restaurant or home in Karnataka.
constraint_note: Write each word in Kannada script if possible. Say aloud. Try to use at least one at your next meal.
example: ಅನ್ನ (Anna) = rice, ರೊಟ್ಟಿ (Rotti) = bread/roti, ಸಾರು (Saaru) = rasam, ಮೊಸರು (Mosaru) = curd/yogurt.

details.label: Use
details.value: Swalpa anna kodi = Give some rice

items.body: Pronounced UN-na with a short 'u' like 'put'. Rice is the staple food of Karnataka — most meals revolve around it. 'Anna' appears in countless phrases: 'anna-saaru' (rice with rasam), 'bisi-bele-bath' (hot lentil rice). In restaurants, just saying 'anna' gets you a plate of steamed rice. The word is so fundamental that refusing rice ('anna beda') is almost a cultural statement.

details.label: Use
details.value: Rotti bisi aagide = The roti is hot

items.body: Pronounced ROT-ti with a rolled 'r' and doubled 't'. In Karnataka, rotti specifically means jowar (sorghum) or ragi (finger millet) flatbread — not the wheat roti of North India. 'Ragi rotti' is a Karnataka specialty: dark, nutty, and incredibly nutritious. Asking for 'rotti' in a local restaurant signals you know the regional cuisine.

details.label: Use
details.value: Saaru tumba ruchi = The rasam is very tasty

items.body: Pronounced SAA-ru with a long 'aa'. Saaru is Karnataka's version of rasam — a thin, tangy, spiced soup made with tamarind and tomato. It's poured over rice and eaten with the hands. Every family has their own saaru recipe, and it's often the first dish children learn to make. 'Saaru anna' (rasam rice) is comfort food for most Kannadigas.

Pronounced HU-li. Means 'sour/tamarind dish.' Huli is a thick, tangy curry made with tamarind — a Karnataka staple served with rice. 'Huli anna' (tamarind rice) is comfort food. The word also means 'sour' as an adjective: 'ee hannu huli' = this fruit is sour.

Pronounced MO-sa-ru. Curd/yogurt — essential in Karnataka cuisine. 'Mosaru anna' (curd rice) is the traditional last course of a meal, believed to cool the stomach. 'Mosaru bajji' is a popular snack. Curd is so important that 'mosaru kodthini' (I'll give you curd) is a common hospitality phrase.

details.label: Use
details.value: Swalpa neeru kodi = Give some water

items.body: Pronounced NEE-ru with a long 'ee'. Water — one of the first words you need. 'Swalpa neeru kodi' = please give some water. 'Neeru' appears in place names: Shivanasamudra (Shiva's sea of water). In restaurants, just saying 'neeru' gets you a glass of water.

details.label: Use
details.value: Bisi kaapi beku = I want hot coffee

items.body: Pronounced KAA-fi, borrowed from English 'coffee.' Karnataka is India's largest coffee producer, and filter coffee is a cultural institution. 'Ondu kaafi kodi' = give one coffee. Bangalore's coffee culture predates the Starbucks era by decades — every street has a 'kaafi' stall.

details.label: Use
details.value: Ondu chai kodi = Give one tea

items.body: Pronounced CHA-haa. Tea — though many Kannadigas also use 'tea' directly from English. 'Chaha beku?' = do you want tea? Karnataka's Malnad region produces excellent tea. In most offices and homes, 'chaha time' is a social ritual, not just a beverage break.

Pronounced HUN-ni-na RA-sa. Literally 'fruit juice.' 'Hannina' = of fruit, 'rasa' = juice/essence. Fresh juice stalls are everywhere in Karnataka — 'mosambhi rasa' (sweet lime juice) is the most popular. 'Rasa' also means 'taste' or 'essence' in a poetic sense.

Pronounced UP-pi-na-KAA-yi. Literally 'salt-fruit' — pickled vegetables/fruits preserved in salt, oil, and spices. Every Karnataka household has homemade uppinakayi. Mango pickle ('maavina uppinakayi') is the most common. It's served as a side with every meal and is a point of family pride.

narrator_action: Learn these food words: Anna (rice), Rotti (bread), Saaru (rasam), Huli (sambar), Mosaru (curd), Neeru (water), Kaapi (coffee), Chai (tea), Hannina Rasa (juice), Uppinakayi (pickle).

description: Practice the basic Kannada sentence pattern: Subject + Object + Verb. Kannada is an SOV language — the verb always comes last.
action: Learn the SOV pattern. Form 5 simple sentences: 'I water drink' = Naanu neeru kudiyuttene.
context: Kannada word order is Subject-Object-Verb, like Hindi and unlike English. Once you internalize this, you can start forming your own sentences.
constraint_note: Write 5 sentences following the SOV pattern. Use words you already know. Say each aloud.
example: ನಾನು ನೀರು ಕುಡಿಯುತ್ತೇನೆ (Naanu neeru kudiyuttene) = I water drink = I drink water. ನಾನು ಅನ್ನ ತಿನ್ನುತ್ತೇನೆ (Naanu anna tinnuttene) = I eat rice.
narrator_action: Learn the SOV pattern. Form 5 simple sentences: 'I water drink' = Naanu neeru kudiyuttene.

description: Learn 10 body part words in Kannada. Point to each body part as you say the word — physical association helps memory.
action: Learn: Tala (head), Kannu (eye), Moogu (nose), Baayi (mouth), Kivi (ear), Kai (hand), Kaalu (leg), Beral (finger), Hotte (stomach), Benna (back).
context: Body vocabulary is useful for medical situations, giving directions ('right hand side'), and everyday conversation.
constraint_note: Point to each body part as you say the word. Do 3 rounds: look at the list, then try without looking, then test yourself randomly.
example: ತಲೆ (Tala)=head, ಕಣ್ಣು (Kannu)=eye, ಕೈ (Kai)=hand, ಕಾಲು (Kaalu)=leg. Touch each one as you say it.

items.body: Pronounced TA-le. The 't' is a dental 't' — tongue touches the back of your upper teeth, not the ridge behind them like English 't'. 'Tale novu' (headache) is one of the most useful medical phrases. In Karnataka culture, touching someone's head (especially an elder's) is a sign of deep respect — 'tale mele kai' (hand on head) is a blessing gesture.

Pronounced KUN-nu. The double 'n' is held slightly longer. 'Kannu' appears in many expressions: 'kannu haaku' (to cast an eye/evil eye), 'kannu muchchu' (close your eyes). Kannada has a rich vocabulary for eye-related expressions because eyes are culturally significant — 'kannina bombe' (pupil of the eye) literally means 'doll of the eye.'

Pronounced MOO-gu. The long 'oo' distinguishes it from other words. Nose rings ('mookuthi') are traditional in Karnataka — the word comes from 'moogu' + 'uthi' (ring). 'Moogu mele kopa' (anger on the nose) is an idiom meaning someone is visibly angry — because nostrils flare when you're upset.

Pronounced BAA-yi. Mouth. 'Baayi muchchu' = shut your mouth (rude). 'Baayi ruchi' = tasty (literally 'mouth taste'). The idiom 'baayi bittkondu' means 'with mouth open' — expressing surprise. In polite speech, 'baayi' is used in food contexts: 'baayi-ge hittide' = it suits the mouth/palate.

Pronounced KI-vi. Ear. 'Kivi kodu' = listen (literally 'give ear'). 'Kivi matu' = whispered words/gossip. Kannada has a rich set of ear-related idioms: 'kivi hinde' = behind the ear (secretly), 'kivi tumbide' = ears are full (tired of hearing something).

Pronounced KAI (rhymes with 'eye'). Hand. One of the most versatile words — 'kai kodu' = give a hand/help, 'kai bidu' = let go, 'kai haaku' = put your hand (touch). Eating with hands is traditional: 'kai-inda tinnu' = eat with hands. 'Kai' also means 'arm' in casual usage.

Pronounced KAA-lu. Leg/foot. 'Kaalu novu' = leg pain. Touching elders' feet ('kaalu mutthu') is a sign of deep respect in Karnataka. 'Kaalu haaku' = step in/enter. The word covers both 'leg' and 'foot' — context makes it clear which is meant.

Pronounced BE-ra-la. Finger. 'Kai beralu' = hand finger, 'kaalu beralu' = toe (foot finger). 'Beralu torshu' = point (show finger). Counting on fingers is universal, and Kannada has specific words for each finger, though 'beralu' is the general term used most often.

Pronounced HOT-te. Stomach/belly. 'Hotte hasivu' = hungry (stomach hunger). 'Hotte tumba' = stomach full. One of the most useful words for daily life — 'hotte novu' = stomachache. The idiom 'hotte urityde' = stomach is burning (very hungry or jealous).

Pronounced BEN-na. Back (body part). 'Benna novu' = back pain. 'Benna hinde' = behind someone's back. 'Benna thattu' = pat on the back (encouragement). In Karnataka's agricultural communities, 'benna' is associated with hard work — 'benna mugidu' = back is broken (exhausted from labor).

narrator_action: Learn: Tala (head), Kannu (eye), Moogu (nose), Baayi (mouth), Kivi (ear), Kai (hand), Kaalu (leg), Beral (finger), Hotte (stomach), Benna (back).

description: Learn 10 common household object words in Kannada. Look at each object in your home as you learn the word.
action: Learn: Mane (house), Baagilu (door), Kitiki (window), Kurchi (chair), Meja (table), Haasi (bed), Deepa (lamp), Batte (clothes), Cheela (bag), Gudisalu (broom).
context: Household words let you start thinking in Kannada at home. Labeling objects mentally in Kannada builds passive vocabulary fast.
constraint_note: Look at each object as you say the word. Try mentally labeling 5 objects in your room right now in Kannada.
example: ಮನೆ (Mane)=house, ಬಾಗಿಲು (Baagilu)=door, ಕಿಟಿಕಿ (Kitiki)=window. Look at your door and say 'Baagilu' three times.

items.body: Pronounced MA-ne. One of the most important words — 'mane' means both 'house' and 'home.' 'Mane-ge hogu' = go home. 'Mane kelsa' = housework. In Kannada culture, 'mane' carries emotional weight — 'namma mane' (our home) implies family, belonging, and identity. Real estate ads in Bangalore still use 'mane' rather than English 'house' for emotional appeal.

Pronounced BAA-gi-lu. Three syllables with a long 'aa'. 'Baagilu tegeyi' = open the door. 'Baagilu hakko' = close the door (literally 'put the door'). The word 'hakko' for closing is interesting — Kannada uses 'put' where English uses 'close.' This kind of verb difference is where direct translation breaks down and you need to think in Kannada.

Pronounced KI-ta-ki. Window. A fun, rhythmic word. 'Kitaki tegeyi' = open the window. 'Kitaki hakko' = close the window. In traditional Karnataka homes, windows were small to keep out heat; modern apartments have larger ones.

Pronounced KUR-chi. Chair — borrowed from Urdu/Hindi. 'Kurchi mele kootko' = sit on the chair. In traditional Karnataka homes, sitting on the floor was common; 'kurchi' represents modern furniture. 'Kurchi kodu' = give a chair (offer a seat to a guest).

Pronounced MAY-ja. Table — borrowed from Portuguese 'mesa' via Hindi. 'Meja mele ittu' = put it on the table. Like 'kurchi,' this is a borrowed word reflecting how furniture culture came to India. Traditional meals are served on the floor on banana leaves.

Pronounced HAA-si. Bed/mattress. 'Haasi haasu' = spread the bed. In many Karnataka homes, beds are rolled-up mattresses spread on the floor at night. 'Haasi mele malako' = lie down on the bed. The word also means 'to spread' — 'haasi' is both the noun and related to the verb.

Pronounced DEE-pa. Lamp/light. 'Deepa hachu' = light the lamp. Lighting a lamp is central to Karnataka culture — every puja (prayer) begins with it. 'Deepavali' (Diwali) literally means 'row of lamps.' 'Deepa' carries spiritual significance beyond just illumination.

Pronounced BUT-te. Clothes/cloth. 'Batte ogeyodu' = wash clothes. 'Hosa batte' = new clothes. Buying new clothes for festivals is a major tradition — 'habba batte' = festival clothes. 'Batte angadi' = cloth shop. The word covers both the material and the garment.

Pronounced CHEE-la. Bag. 'Cheela togo' = take the bag. 'Shaale cheela' = school bag. After Karnataka's plastic bag ban, 'batte cheela' (cloth bag) became common. The word is straightforward with no tricky pronunciation — one of the easier vocabulary items.

Pronounced GU-di-sa-lu. Broom. 'Gudisalu kodu' = give the broom. 'Gudisu' is the verb 'to sweep.' In many households, sweeping is the first morning chore. 'Mane gudisu' = sweep the house. The broom is traditionally made from coconut palm fronds in coastal Karnataka.

narrator_action: Learn: Mane (house), Baagilu (door), Kitiki (window), Kurchi (chair), Meja (table), Haasi (bed), Deepa (lamp), Batte (clothes), Cheela (bag), Gudisalu (broom).

description: Without looking at any reference, write down all the Kannada numbers 1-10. Then check yourself. Which ones did you forget?
action: From memory, write Kannada numbers 1-10 in both script and transliteration. Then check against your notes.
context: Retrieval practice — testing yourself from memory — is proven to be more effective than re-reading. The struggle to remember is where learning happens.
constraint_note: No peeking. Write what you remember first. Then check. The ones you forgot are the ones to practice next.
example: Try it now: 1=?, 2=?, 3=?... If you got 7/10, that's great. Focus your next practice on the 3 you missed.

action: Learn the 'ta' group consonants: ಟ ಠ ಡ ಢ ಣ. Write each 5 times.
description: Learn the 'ta' (retroflex) group consonants: ಟ ಠ ಡ ಢ ಣ. These are retroflex sounds — tongue curls back. Write each 5 times.
context: Third row of consonants — retroflex sounds. These don't exist in English but are common in Kannada. The tongue position is key.
constraint_note: Write and say aloud. Feel your tongue curl back for each sound. Compare with the dental group (ತ ಥ ದ ಧ ನ) which you'll learn next.
example: ಟ = 'ta' (retroflex), ಠ = 'tha' (retroflex), ಡ = 'da' (retroflex), ಢ = 'dha' (retroflex), ಣ = 'na' (retroflex).

action: Learn the dental group: ತ ಥ ದ ಧ ನ. Write each 5 times.
description: Learn the dental consonants: ತ ಥ ದ ಧ ನ. These are made with the tongue touching the teeth. Write each 5 times.
context: Fourth row — dental sounds. Your tongue touches the back of your upper teeth. Compare with retroflex (ಟ group) where the tongue curls back.
constraint_note: Write and say aloud. Alternate between ಟ (retroflex) and ತ (dental) to feel the difference.
example: ತ = 'ta' (dental), ಥ = 'tha' (dental), ದ = 'da' (dental), ಧ = 'dha' (dental), ನ = 'na' (dental).

action: Learn the labial group: ಪ ಫ ಬ ಭ ಮ. Write each 5 times.
description: Learn the labial consonants: ಪ ಫ ಬ ಭ ಮ. These are lip sounds. Write each 5 times and say aloud.
context: Fifth and final row of the main consonant grid. Labial = lips. After this, you know the core 25 consonants.
constraint_note: Write and say aloud. After finishing, try writing all 5 consonant rows from memory (ka, cha, ta-retroflex, ta-dental, pa).
example: ಪ = 'pa', ಫ = 'pha', ಬ = 'ba', ಭ = 'bha', ಮ = 'ma'. You use your lips for all of these.

action: Learn 10 direction and location words: Bala (right), Edakke (left), Nere (straight), Mele (up/above), Kelage (down/below), Mundhe (front), Hinde (behind), Hattira (near), Doora (far), Madhya (middle).
description: Learn 10 direction words in Kannada. These are essential for navigation, giving/receiving directions, and auto rides.
context: Direction words are survival vocabulary in a new city. Combined with auto phrases, these let you navigate Bangalore in Kannada.
constraint_note: Point in each direction as you say the word. Practice a mini-direction: 'Nere hogi, bala tiru' (Go straight, turn right).
example: ಬಲ (Bala)=right, ಎಡಕ್ಕೆ (Edakke)=left, ನೇರ (Nere)=straight. Try: 'Nere hogi, edakke tiru, hattira nilsi' = Go straight, turn left, stop nearby.

items.body: Pronounced BA-la. Right (direction). 'Bala pakka tiru' = turn right side. Memory aid: 'bala' also means 'strength' — your right hand is usually your strong hand. In auto-rickshaw navigation: 'mundina bala tiru' = take the next right.

Pronounced E-dak-ke. Left (direction). 'Edakke tiru' = turn left. The '-kke' suffix indicates direction/toward. Without it, 'eda' just means 'left side.' Kannada directions are simpler than English — no 'take a left' vs 'turn left' distinction.

Pronounced NAY-ra. Straight ahead. 'Nera hogi' = go straight. One of the most useful navigation words. Auto drivers understand 'nera, nera' (straight, straight) even with imperfect pronunciation. Also means 'direct' or 'honest' — 'nera manusha' = straightforward person.

Pronounced MAY-le. Up/above/on top. 'Mele hogi' = go up. 'Table mele' = on the table. The '-le' suffix indicates location. 'Mele mane' = upstairs house/upper floor. In Bangalore's hilly terrain, 'mele' and 'kelage' (below) are essential for giving directions.

Pronounced KE-la-ge. Down/below. 'Kelage banni' = come down. 'Kelage idu' = put it down. Opposite of 'mele.' In multi-story buildings: 'kelage hogi' = go downstairs. The word also means 'under' — 'table kelage' = under the table.

Pronounced MUN-dhe. Front/ahead. 'Mundhe hogi' = go forward. 'Mundhe nodu' = look ahead. 'Mundhina' = next (the one ahead). Very useful in navigation: 'mundhina cross hattira' = near the next crossing. Also used for time: 'mundhina vaara' = next week.

Pronounced HIN-dhe. Behind/back. 'Hinde nodu' = look behind. 'Hinde banni' = come back. Opposite of 'mundhe.' 'Hindhina' = previous. 'Hinde-mundhina' = back and forth. In traffic: 'hinde vehicle ide' = there's a vehicle behind.

Pronounced HAT-ti-ra. Near/close. 'Hattira ide' = it's nearby. 'Yeshtu hattira?' = how near? Very useful for asking about distances. 'Hattira banni' = come closer. In Bangalore, 'hattira' is relative — '10 minutes hattira' could mean 30 minutes in traffic.

Pronounced DOO-ra. Far/distant. 'Tumba doora' = very far. 'Yeshtu doora?' = how far? Essential for auto-rickshaw negotiations — knowing if something is 'hattira' or 'doora' helps you judge if the fare is reasonable. 'Doora-darshana' = television (literally 'far-seeing').

Pronounced MAD-hya. Middle/center. 'Madhya-dalli' = in the middle. 'Road madhya' = middle of the road. Also used figuratively: 'madhyastha' = mediator (one who stands in the middle). 'Madhya Pradesh' (the Indian state) literally means 'middle province.'

narrator_action: Learn 10 direction and location words: Bala (right), Edakke (left), Nere (straight), Mele (up/above), Kelage (down/below), Mundhe (front), Hinde (behind), Hattira (near), Doora (far), Madhya (middle).

action: Learn 10 time-related words: Ivattu (today), Ninne (yesterday), Naale (tomorrow), Beligge (morning), Madhyahna (afternoon), Saanje (evening), Raatri (night), Gante (hour), Nimisha (minute), Vaara (week).
description: Learn 10 time words in Kannada. Time vocabulary is essential for making plans, appointments, and understanding schedules.
context: Time words let you participate in planning conversations. 'Naale beligge' (tomorrow morning) is immediately useful.
constraint_note: Write each word. Make 3 time phrases: 'Ivattu saanje' (this evening), 'Naale beligge' (tomorrow morning), 'Ninne raatri' (last night).
example: ಇವತ್ತು (Ivattu)=today, ನಿನ್ನೆ (Ninne)=yesterday, ನಾಳೆ (Naale)=tomorrow. Try: 'Naale saanje sigona?' = Shall we meet tomorrow evening?

items.body: Pronounced I-vat-tu. Today. 'Ivattu enu maadona?' = what shall we do today? One of the most common time words. 'Ivattu raatre' = tonight. The double 't' is important — hold it slightly longer. In casual speech, often shortened to 'ivattu' or even 'ivatt.'

Pronounced NIN-ne. Yesterday. 'Ninne enu aaytu?' = what happened yesterday? Simple and frequently used. 'Ninne raatre' = last night. 'Monne' = day before yesterday — Kannada has a specific word for this, unlike English which needs three words.

Pronounced NAA-le. Tomorrow. 'Naale sigona' = see you tomorrow. 'Naale dina' = tomorrow's day. 'Naaliddu' = day after tomorrow — again, Kannada has a single word where English needs three. These time words (ninne, ivattu, naale) are among the first you should memorize.

narrator_action: Learn 10 time-related words: Ivattu (today), Ninne (yesterday), Naale (tomorrow), Beligge (morning), Madhyahna (afternoon), Saanje (evening), Raatri (night), Gante (hour), Nimisha (minute), Vaara (week).

action: Learn past tense: verb root + de (I), dya (you-informal), ri (you-formal), da (he), lu (she). Conjugate: maadu, hogi, tinnu (eat).
description: Learn past tense verb conjugation. The pattern: verb root + de/dya/ri/da/lu. Practice with 3 common verbs.
context: Past tense lets you talk about what happened — 'I went,' 'she ate,' 'we came.' It's the second most useful tense after present.
constraint_note: Write all forms for 3 verbs. Say each aloud. Try making a sentence: 'Naanu ninne Majestic-ge hogde' (I went to Majestic yesterday).
example: ಹೋಗು (hogu=go): Naanu hogde (I went), Avanu hogda (he went), Avalu hoglu (she went). Naanu anna tinde (I ate rice).
narrator_action: Learn past tense: verb root + de (I), dya (you-informal), ri (you-formal), da (he), lu (she). Conjugate: maadu, hogi, tinnu (eat).

action: From memory, write all 14 Kannada vowels. Then check yourself.
description: Test yourself: write all 14 Kannada vowels from memory. No peeking. Then check and note which ones you missed.
context: Retrieval practice strengthens memory more than re-reading. The effort of trying to remember — even when you fail — is the learning.
constraint_note: Write first, check after. Circle the ones you got wrong. Those are your focus for next time.
example: Try now: ಅ ಆ ಇ ಈ ಉ ಊ ಋ ಎ ಏ ಐ ಒ ಓ ಔ + ಅಂ ಅಃ. How many did you get?

action: Learn 10 color words: Kempu (red), Neeli (blue), Hasiru (green), Haladi (yellow), Bili (white), Kappu (black), Kittale (orange), Neerali (purple), Gulaabi (pink), Boodhu (grey).
description: Learn 10 color words in Kannada. Colors come up in shopping, descriptions, and daily conversation.
context: Color vocabulary is useful for shopping ('Kempu batte kodi' = give me the red cloth) and describing things around you.
constraint_note: Point to something of each color as you say the word. Make 3 phrases: 'Kempu car,' 'Hasiru mara (green tree),' 'Neeli aakasha (blue sky).'
example: ಕೆಂಪು (Kempu)=red, ನೀಲಿ (Neeli)=blue, ಹಸಿರು (Hasiru)=green, ಬಿಳಿ (Bili)=white, ಕಪ್ಪು (Kappu)=black.

items.body: Pronounced KEM-pu. Red — associated with auspiciousness in Karnataka culture. 'Kempu' appears in place names: Kempe Gowda (Bangalore's founder), Kempegowda Bus Station. Brides wear red ('kempu seere' = red sari). The kumkum (red dot) on the forehead is 'kempu bottu.' Knowing color words helps in shopping — 'kempu banna beku' = I want red color.

Pronounced NEE-li. Blue — a straightforward word. 'Neeli aakaasha' = blue sky. Karnataka's state bird, the Indian Roller, is called 'neelakantha' (blue-throated one). In textile shopping, 'neeli' covers all shades of blue — for specific shades, you'd add qualifiers like 'light neeli' or 'dark neeli.'

Pronounced HA-si-ru. Green — literally related to 'hasi' meaning 'fresh/raw.' 'Hasiru' describes both the color and freshness: 'hasiru tarkari' = fresh/green vegetables. Karnataka's lush Western Ghats are described as 'hasiru swarga' (green paradise). The connection between green and freshness is built into the language itself.

Pronounced HA-la-di. Yellow — related to 'haladi' (turmeric), which is yellow. Turmeric is sacred in Karnataka — 'haladi kumkuma' is a women's ceremony. 'Haladi banna' = yellow color. The connection between the spice and the color is built into the language.

Pronounced BI-li. White. 'Bili batte' = white clothes. White is associated with mourning in Karnataka (unlike Western culture where it's black). 'Bili akki' = white rice. 'Bili moda' = white cloud. A simple, common word with no tricky pronunciation.

Pronounced KAP-pu. Black. 'Kappu kaafi' = black coffee. 'Kappu banna' = black color. The doubled 'p' is important. 'Kappu' can also describe dark complexion, though this usage is becoming less common as awareness of colorism grows.

Pronounced KIT-ta-le. Orange — literally the name of the orange fruit. Like English, the color is named after the fruit. 'Kittale hannu' = orange fruit. 'Kittale banna' = orange color. The doubled 't' is characteristic of Kannada.

Pronounced NAY-ra-le. Purple — related to 'nerale' (jamun/java plum), a dark purple fruit common in Karnataka. The fruit gives its name to the color, similar to how 'orange' works in English. 'Nerale hannu' is a beloved seasonal fruit in Karnataka.

Pronounced GU-laa-bi. Pink — literally 'rose' (from the rose flower). 'Gulaabi banna' = pink color. Borrowed from Persian/Urdu. 'Gulaabi' is used for both the flower and the color. 'Gulaabi jamoon' is a popular sweet — rose-colored milk dumplings.

Pronounced BOO-dhu. Grey — related to 'boodi' (ash). 'Boodhu banna' = grey color. 'Boodhu aakaasha' = grey sky (overcast). Less commonly used than primary colors but useful for describing weather, hair ('boodhu koodalu' = grey hair), and clothing.

narrator_action: Learn 10 color words: Kempu (red), Neeli (blue), Hasiru (green), Haladi (yellow), Bili (white), Kappu (black), Kittale (orange), Neerali (purple), Gulaabi (pink), Boodhu (grey).

action: Learn 10 weather and nature words: Mazhe (rain), Bisilu (sun/heat), Gaali (wind), Mooda (cloud), Mara (tree), Hoovu (flower), Hulla (grass), Betta (hill), Nadi (river), Kere (lake).
description: Learn 10 nature and weather words. Karnataka has diverse geography — these words come up constantly.
context: Weather is universal small talk. 'Ivattu tumba bisilu' (very hot today) or 'Mazhe baratte' (rain is coming) are everyday phrases.
constraint_note: Make 3 weather sentences: 'Ivattu mazhe baratte' (rain is coming today), 'Tumba bisilu' (very hot), 'Chennagirodu gaali' (nice wind).
example: ಮಳೆ (Mazhe)=rain, ಬಿಸಿಲು (Bisilu)=sun/heat, ಗಾಳಿ (Gaali)=wind. 'Ivattu mazhe banditu' = It rained today.

items.body: Pronounced MA-zhe. Rain — one of the most important words in Karnataka, where monsoons define the agricultural calendar. 'Mazhe bartide' = it's raining (rain is coming). 'Mazhe kaala' = rainy season. Bangalore's famous pleasant weather is described as 'mazhe haava' (rainy climate).

Pronounced BI-si-lu. Sun/heat/sunshine. 'Bisilu jaasti' = too much sun. 'Bisilu hotta' = sunstroke. In summer, 'bisilu' is the most-discussed topic. 'Bisilu kudure' = mirage (literally 'sun horse'). 'Bisilu-mazzhe' = sun and rain together — considered auspicious.

Pronounced GAA-li. Wind/air. 'Gaali bartide' = wind is blowing. 'Tanna gaali' = cool breeze. 'Gaali pata' = kite (wind kite). Also means 'air' in general: 'gaali illade' = no air/stuffy. 'Gaali maatu' = empty talk (just wind/air).

narrator_action: Learn 10 weather and nature words: Mazhe (rain), Bisilu (sun/heat), Gaali (wind), Mooda (cloud), Mara (tree), Hoovu (flower), Hulla (grass), Betta (hill), Nadi (river), Kere (lake).

action: Learn to make negative sentences. Pattern: verb root + alla (not). Naanu hogalla (I won't go), Idu chennagilla (This is not good), Nanage gotilla (I don't know).
description: Learn negation in Kannada. Adding 'alla' or 'illa' makes sentences negative.
context: Saying 'no' and 'not' is as important as saying 'yes.' Negation lets you refuse, disagree, and clarify — essential for real conversation.
constraint_note: Take 5 positive sentences you know and make them negative. Say both versions aloud.
example: ನಾನು ಹೋಗುತ್ತೇನೆ (I go) → ನಾನು ಹೋಗಲ್ಲ (I won't go). ಇದು ಚೆನ್ನಾಗಿದೆ (This is good) → ಇದು ಚೆನ್ನಾಗಿಲ್ಲ (This is not good).
narrator_action: Learn to make negative sentences. Pattern: verb root + alla (not). Naanu hogalla (I won't go), Idu chennagilla (This is not good), Nanage gotilla (I don't know).

action: From memory, write all the food words you've learned in Kannada. Then write all the body part words. Check yourself.
description: Test yourself: write all Kannada food words and body part words from memory. No peeking.
context: Retrieval practice across categories tests whether vocabulary is sticking. The words you can't recall are the ones that need more practice.
constraint_note: Write food words first, then body parts. Check after each category. Note which ones you missed.
example: Food: Anna, Rotti, Saaru, Huli, Mosaru... Body: Tala, Kannu, Moogu... How many did you get?

action: Learn the semi-vowels and sibilants: ಯ ರ ಲ ವ ಶ ಷ ಸ ಹ ಳ. Write each 5 times.
description: Learn the remaining Kannada consonants: semi-vowels (ya, ra, la, va) and sibilants (sha, Sha, sa, ha, La). These complete the alphabet.
context: With these 9 letters, you know the complete Kannada alphabet — 14 vowels + 25 consonants + 9 additional = 48 base characters.
constraint_note: Write each 5 times. After finishing, celebrate — you can now recognize every base letter in Kannada script.
example: ಯ=ya, ರ=ra, ಲ=la, ವ=va, ಶ=sha, ಷ=Sha (retroflex), ಸ=sa, ಹ=ha, ಳ=La (retroflex). You now know the full alphabet.

action: Learn 10 transport words: Bus, Auto, Car (kaaru), Train (railu), Cycle (saikalu), Bike, Nimbu (bus stop), Station, Ticket, Sadak/Raste (road).
description: Learn 10 transport-related words. These combine with direction words for navigation.
context: Transport vocabulary + direction words + auto phrases = you can navigate anywhere in Karnataka in Kannada.
constraint_note: Make 3 sentences: 'Bus nimbu yelli?' (Where's the bus stop?), 'Auto beku' (I need an auto), 'Station-ge eshtu doora?' (How far is the station?).
example: ಆಟೋ ಬೇಕು (Auto beku)=I need an auto. ಬಸ್ ನಿಲ್ದಾಣ ಎಲ್ಲಿ? (Bus nildaana yelli?)=Where's the bus stop?

action: Learn to express wants and needs: Beku (want/need), Beda (don't want). Practice: Nanage neeru beku (I need water), Nanage idu beda (I don't want this).
description: Learn 'beku' (want/need) and 'beda' (don't want) — two of the most useful words in daily Kannada.
context: Beku and beda are survival words. With just these two + nouns, you can express most basic needs.
constraint_note: Make 5 'beku' sentences and 5 'beda' sentences using vocabulary you know. Say each aloud.
example: ನನಗೆ ಕಾಫಿ ಬೇಕು (Nanage kaapi beku)=I want coffee. ನನಗೆ ಸಕ್ಕರೆ ಬೇಡ (Nanage sakkare beda)=I don't want sugar.
narrator_action: Learn to express wants and needs: Beku (want/need), Beda (don't want). Practice: Nanage neeru beku (I need water), Nanage idu beda (I don't want this).

action: Learn 10 emotion/feeling words: Khushi (happy), Dukha (sad), Kopa (angry), Bhaya (fear), Aashcharya (surprise), Preeti (love), Bejaar (bored/upset), Hasivu (hunger), Nidde (sleep), Aayaasa (tired).
description: Learn 10 emotion and feeling words. Expressing how you feel is essential for real conversation beyond transactions.
context: Emotion words move you from transactional Kannada ('how much?') to personal Kannada ('I'm happy,' 'I'm tired'). This is where real connection starts.
constraint_note: Make sentences: 'Nanage tumba khushi aagide' (I'm very happy), 'Nanage hasivu aagide' (I'm hungry), 'Naanu aayaasa aagiddene' (I'm tired).
example: ಖುಷಿ (Khushi)=happy, ದುಃಖ (Dukha)=sad, ಕೋಪ (Kopa)=angry. 'Nanage tumba khushi aagide' = I'm very happy.

items.body: Pronounced KHU-shi. Happy/happiness. 'Tumba khushi' = very happy. Borrowed from Urdu/Hindi but fully naturalized in Kannada. 'Khushi aaytu' = became happy. Used constantly: 'nimage khushi aagide?' = are you happy? A warm, expressive word.

Pronounced DUH-kha. Sad/sorrow. A Sanskrit-origin word used across Indian languages. 'Dukha aagide' = feeling sad. 'Dukha pattkondu' = don't feel sad. More formal than casual speech — in everyday Kannada, people might say 'bejaar' (upset) instead.

Pronounced KO-pa. Angry/anger. 'Kopa bartide' = getting angry. 'Kopa maadkobedi' = don't get angry. 'Kopa-tapa' = anger and frustration (a common pairing). 'Kopishta' = angry person. A very useful word for understanding emotional conversations.

narrator_action: Learn 10 emotion/feeling words: Khushi (happy), Dukha (sad), Kopa (angry), Bhaya (fear), Aashcharya (surprise), Preeti (love), Bejaar (bored/upset), Hasivu (hunger), Nidde (sleep), Aayaasa (tired).

action: Practice reading Kannada signs. Write these common sign words and practice recognizing them: ನಿಲ್ಲಿ (Stop), ಹೋಗಿ (Go), ಪ್ರವೇಶ (Entry), ನಿರ್ಗಮನ (Exit), ಶೌಚಾಲಯ (Toilet), ಅಪಾಯ (Danger).
description: Learn to read 6 common Kannada sign words. These appear on roads, buildings, and public spaces across Karnataka.
context: Reading signs is the first practical use of script knowledge. Even recognizing a few words on signs builds confidence and safety.
constraint_note: Write each word 3 times. Next time you're outside, try to spot and read Kannada signs.
example: ನಿಲ್ಲಿ (Nilli)=Stop, ಪ್ರವೇಶ (Pravesha)=Entry, ನಿರ್ಗಮನ (Nirgamana)=Exit. Look for these on your next walk.

action: Learn 10 workplace words: Kelsa (work), Office, Meeting, Phone, Computer, Email, Boss (yajamana), Colleague (sahodyogi), Salary (sambala), Holiday (rajadinа/chutti).
description: Learn 10 workplace-related words. Even if your office uses English, knowing these helps with Kannada-speaking colleagues and staff.
context: Workplace Kannada bridges the gap between 'survival phrases' and 'real life in Karnataka.' Using even a few Kannada words at work changes relationships.
constraint_note: Make 3 work sentences: 'Naanu kelsa maaduttiddene' (I'm working), 'Ivattu meeting ide' (There's a meeting today), 'Naale chutti' (Tomorrow is holiday).
example: ಕೆಲಸ (Kelsa)=work, ಸಂಬಳ (Sambala)=salary, ಚುಟ್ಟಿ (Chutti)=holiday. 'Tumba kelsa ide' = There's a lot of work.

items.body: Pronounced KE-la-sa. Work/job. 'Kelsa maadu' = do work. 'Kelsa-kke hogbeku' = need to go to work. 'Kelsa illa' = no work/unemployed. One of the most common words in daily conversation. 'Enu kelsa?' = what work? (what do you do for a living?).

Pronounced SAM-ba-la. Salary/wages. 'Sambala bandide' = salary has come. 'Yeshtu sambala?' = how much salary? A practical word for professional contexts. 'Sambala dina' = payday. In casual conversation, asking about salary is more acceptable in India than in Western cultures.

Pronounced CHUT-ti. Holiday/leave. 'Chutti beku' = need a holiday. 'Chutti dina' = holiday/day off. 'Chutti haaku' = take leave. Borrowed from Hindi. 'Yeshtu dina chutti?' = how many days off? Essential for workplace conversations.

narrator_action: Learn 10 workplace words: Kelsa (work), Office, Meeting, Phone, Computer, Email, Boss (yajamana), Colleague (sahodyogi), Salary (sambala), Holiday (rajadinа/chutti).

action: Learn 10 animal words: Nayi (dog), Bekku (cat), Haasu (cow), Kudure (horse), Aane (elephant), Huli (tiger), Haavu (snake), Hakki (bird), Meenu (fish), Kooli (chicken).
description: Learn 10 animal words in Kannada. Animals come up in stories, idioms, and daily life in Karnataka.
context: Animal words appear in Kannada proverbs and everyday speech. 'Nayi' and 'Bekku' are especially common — street dogs and cats are everywhere.
constraint_note: Write each word. Make 3 sentences: 'Nayi baruttide' (dog is coming), 'Bekku maneyalli ide' (cat is in the house), 'Aane tumba doddu' (elephant is very big).
example: ನಾಯಿ (Nayi)=dog, ಬೆಕ್ಕು (Bekku)=cat, ಹಸು (Haasu)=cow, ಆನೆ (Aane)=elephant. 'Aa nayi nanna friend' = That dog is my friend.

items.body: Pronounced NAA-yi. Dog. 'Nayi bega bartide' = the dog is coming fast. Street dogs are common in Karnataka — 'beedhi nayi' = street dog. 'Nayi mari' = puppy. The word is simple but important for daily life, especially for navigating streets where dogs are everywhere.

Pronounced BEK-ku. Cat. 'Bekku bandide' = the cat has come. 'Bekku-nayi' = cat and dog (used like 'cats and dogs' in English for fighting). 'Bekku mari' = kitten. Less common as pets than dogs in Karnataka, but the word appears in many proverbs.

Pronounced HA-su. Cow — sacred in Hindu culture and very present in Karnataka life. 'Haasu haalu' = cow's milk. Cows wandering streets is common. 'Haasu' is treated with reverence — 'go-puja' (cow worship) is practiced in rural areas. 'Haasu mane' = cowshed.

Pronounced AA-ne. Elephant — Karnataka's state animal. 'Aane savari' = elephant ride. Mysore Dasara features a famous elephant procession. 'Aane bala' = elephant strength (very strong). Karnataka has the highest elephant population in India. 'Aane haavabhava' = elephant's grace.

narrator_action: Learn 10 animal words: Nayi (dog), Bekku (cat), Haasu (cow), Kudure (horse), Aane (elephant), Huli (tiger), Haavu (snake), Hakki (bird), Meenu (fish), Kooli (chicken).

action: Learn 10 fruit and vegetable words: Hannu (fruit), Maamsa (meat), Bele (dal), Aalu (potato), Eerulli (onion), Bellulli (garlic), Tomato, Mensinakayi (chilli), Baalehannu (banana), Maavinhannu (mango).
description: Learn 10 fruit, vegetable, and ingredient words. Essential for kitchen conversations and market shopping.
context: Market vocabulary lets you shop in Kannada. 'Ondu kg eerulli kodi' (give 1 kg onions) is a real interaction you can have today.
constraint_note: Practice a market interaction: 'Tomato eshtu?' (how much are tomatoes?), 'Eradu kg aalu kodi' (give 2 kg potatoes).
example: ಆಲೂ (Aalu)=potato, ಈರುಳ್ಳಿ (Eerulli)=onion, ಬಾಳೆಹಣ್ಣು (Baalehannu)=banana. 'Maavinhannu tumba chennaagide' = The mango is very good.

items.body: Pronounced AA-loo. Potato — borrowed from Hindi. 'Aalu palya' = potato curry. 'Aalu bonda' = potato fritter (a popular snack). Potatoes are used in almost every Karnataka dish. 'Aalu gedde' = potato tuber. Simple word, essential for ordering food.

Pronounced EE-rul-li. Onion. The retroflex 'ḷḷ' is tricky — curl your tongue back. 'Eerulli sambar' = onion sambar. Onion prices are a major political issue in India — 'eerulli rate' is frequently discussed. 'Eerulli dose' = onion dosa, a breakfast staple.

Pronounced BAA-le-HUN-nu. Banana — literally 'banana fruit.' 'Baale' = banana plant, 'hannu' = fruit. Karnataka is India's largest banana producer. 'Baale ele' = banana leaf (used as plates for meals). 'Baale hannu' is offered in temples and is part of every festival meal.

narrator_action: Learn 10 fruit and vegetable words: Hannu (fruit), Maamsa (meat), Bele (dal), Aalu (potato), Eerulli (onion), Bellulli (garlic), Tomato, Mensinakayi (chilli), Baalehannu (banana), Maavinhannu (mango).

action: Learn how vowel signs (matras) attach to consonants. Practice: ಕ+ಾ=ಕಾ, ಕ+ಿ=ಕಿ, ಕ+ೀ=ಕೀ, ಕ+ು=ಕು, ಕ+ೂ=ಕೂ. Write the full set for ಕ and ಪ.
description: Learn how vowels combine with consonants in Kannada script. This is how most syllables are written.
context: Consonant+vowel combinations are the core of Kannada reading. Once you understand the pattern for one consonant, it works for all of them.
constraint_note: Write all vowel combinations for ಕ (ka, kaa, ki, kee, ku, koo, ke, kay, kai, ko, koh, kou). Then do the same for ಪ.
example: ಕ=ka, ಕಾ=kaa, ಕಿ=ki, ಕೀ=kee, ಕು=ku, ಕೂ=koo, ಕೆ=ke, ಕೇ=kay, ಕೈ=kai, ಕೊ=ko, ಕೋ=koh. Same pattern for every consonant.

action: Practice reading these common Kannada words in script: ಬೆಂಗಳೂರು (Bengaluru), ಕರ್ನಾಟಕ (Karnataka), ನಮಸ್ಕಾರ (Namaskara), ಧನ್ಯವಾದ (Dhanyavada), ಕನ್ನಡ (Kannada).
description: Practice reading 5 common words in Kannada script. These are words you see everywhere — signs, newspapers, TV.
context: Reading familiar words builds script confidence. You already know these words — now recognize them in their written form.
constraint_note: Cover the transliteration. Try to read each word from script alone. Then check. Practice until you can read all 5 without help.
example: ಬೆಂಗಳೂರು — try sounding it out: ಬೆ=be, ಂ=n, ಗ=ga, ಳೂ=loo, ರು=ru → Bengalooru. You can read Kannada!

action: Learn future tense: verb root + ttene (I will), ttiya (you will), ttane (he will), ttale (she will). Conjugate: hogu (go), maadu (do), baa (come).
description: Learn future tense verb conjugation. This lets you talk about plans, intentions, and what will happen.
context: Future tense completes the tense trio — present, past, future. With all three, you can talk about anything in time.
constraint_note: Conjugate 3 verbs in future tense for all pronouns. Make 3 sentences about tomorrow.
example: ನಾನು ನಾಳೆ ಹೋಗುತ್ತೇನೆ (Naanu naale hoguttene)=I will go tomorrow. ಅವಳು ಬರುತ್ತಾಳೆ (Avalu baruttale)=She will come.
narrator_action: Learn future tense: verb root + ttene (I will), ttiya (you will), ttane (he will), ttale (she will). Conjugate: hogu (go), maadu (do), baa (come).

action: Learn 10 adjective words: Doddu (big), Chikku (small), Hosa (new), Halé (old), Bisi (hot), Tanna (cold), Olledu (good), Kettu (bad), Sundara (beautiful), Katina (difficult).
description: Learn 10 common adjectives. Adjectives come before the noun in Kannada, just like English.
context: Adjectives let you describe and compare. 'Doddu mane' (big house), 'Bisi kaapi' (hot coffee) — they make your Kannada more expressive.
constraint_note: Make 5 noun+adjective phrases using objects around you. Say aloud.
example: ದೊಡ್ಡ (Doddu)=big, ಚಿಕ್ಕ (Chikku)=small, ಬಿಸಿ (Bisi)=hot. 'Bisi kaapi beku' = I want hot coffee. 'Doddu mane' = big house.

items.body: Pronounced DOD-da. Big/large. 'Dodda mane' = big house. 'Dodda manusha' = important person (big person). The doubled 'd' is important. 'Doddavaru' = elders/seniors (big people — a respectful term). One of the first adjectives to learn — pairs with 'chikka' (small).

Pronounced CHIK-ka. Small/little. 'Chikka magu' = small child. 'Chikkavaru' = younger ones. Opposite of 'dodda.' 'Chikka-dodda' = big and small (everyone). 'Chikka Bangalore' = old/small Bangalore (the historic city center). A fundamental adjective used constantly.

Pronounced BI-si. Hot (temperature). 'Bisi neeru' = hot water. 'Bisi anna' = hot rice. 'Bisi bisi' = very hot. Don't confuse with 'bisilu' (sunshine). 'Bisi bele bath' = hot lentil rice, a famous Karnataka dish. Opposite is 'tanna' (cold/cool).

narrator_action: Learn 10 adjective words: Doddu (big), Chikku (small), Hosa (new), Halé (old), Bisi (hot), Tanna (cold), Olledu (good), Kettu (bad), Sundara (beautiful), Katina (difficult).

action: Practice writing 10 Kannada words you know from memory in script. No transliteration reference — just the sounds in your head to script on paper.
description: Write 10 Kannada words from memory in script. This tests whether you can convert sounds to written form.
context: Writing from memory — sound to script — is the reverse of reading. Both directions need practice for script to become natural.
constraint_note: Pick 10 words you know well (namaskara, neeru, anna, etc.). Write them in Kannada script from memory. Then check.
example: Try: ನಮಸ್ಕಾರ, ನೀರು, ಅನ್ನ, ಕಾಫಿ, ಮನೆ. How many can you write correctly without looking?

action: Learn 10 common verbs: Maadu (do), Hogu (go), Baa (come), Tinnu (eat), Kudi (drink), Nodu (see/look), Kelu (listen/ask), Helu (tell/say), Odu (read/run), Bareyu (write).
description: Learn 10 essential verbs. These are the most frequently used verbs in daily Kannada.
context: These 10 verbs cover most daily actions. Combined with nouns you know, they let you describe almost anything you do.
constraint_note: For each verb, make one sentence in present tense. Say aloud: 'Naanu pustaka oduttene' (I'm reading a book).
example: ಮಾಡು (Maadu)=do, ಹೋಗು (Hogu)=go, ಬಾ (Baa)=come, ತಿನ್ನು (Tinnu)=eat, ನೋಡು (Nodu)=see. 'Naanu TV noduttene' = I'm watching TV.

items.body: Pronounced MAA-du. Do/make — the most versatile verb in Kannada. 'Enu maadtiddira?' = what are you doing? 'Kelsa maadu' = do work. 'Aduge maadu' = cook (do cooking). Combined with nouns, it creates hundreds of compound verbs. Master this one word and you can express almost any action.

Pronounced HO-gu. Go. 'Mane-ge hogu' = go home. 'Hogona?' = shall we go? 'Hogi banni' = go and come (goodbye — implying you'll return). One of the most essential verbs. Conjugation changes by person: 'naanu hogtini' (I go), 'neenu hogtiya' (you go).

Pronounced BAA. Come — short and sweet, just one syllable. 'Illi baa' = come here. 'Banni' = come (formal/respectful). 'Baa' is informal — use with friends and children. 'Banni' with elders and strangers. The formal/informal distinction in verbs is crucial in Kannada.

Pronounced TIN-nu. Eat. 'Oota tinnu' = eat food. 'Enu tintira?' = what are you eating? 'Tinnu' is informal; 'tinniri' is respectful. Eating is central to Karnataka culture — 'oota aayita?' (have you eaten?) is a common greeting, showing care for the other person.

Pronounced NO-du. See/look. 'Illi nodu' = look here. 'Nodona' = let's see. 'Nodu' is also used as a filler word in conversation, like 'see' or 'look' in English: 'nodu, naanu heLtini...' = see, I'm telling you... Very common in everyday speech.

narrator_action: Learn 10 common verbs: Maadu (do), Hogu (go), Baa (come), Tinnu (eat), Kudi (drink), Nodu (see/look), Kelu (listen/ask), Helu (tell/say), Odu (read/run), Bareyu (write).

action: Category recall: write as many Kannada words as you can for each category — animals, colors, directions, emotions. 2 minutes per category.
description: Timed category recall — write every Kannada word you know in each category. Speed tests whether vocabulary is truly learned.
context: Timed recall simulates real conversation — you need words quickly, not after 30 seconds of thinking. Speed = fluency.
constraint_note: Set a timer. 2 minutes per category: animals, colors, directions, emotions. Write as fast as you can. Count your totals.
example: Animals: nayi, bekku, haasu... Colors: kempu, neeli, hasiru... How many can you get in 2 minutes per category?

action: Learn 10 clothing words: Batte (clothes), Shirt, Pant, Saree, Chudidar, Chappal (slippers), Shoes, Topi (cap), Belt, Kannadaka (spectacles).
description: Learn 10 clothing-related words. Useful for shopping and daily descriptions.
context: Clothing vocabulary is practical for shopping: 'Ee shirt bere banna-alli ide?' (Is this shirt in another color?).
constraint_note: Look at what you're wearing. Name each item in Kannada. Make 2 shopping sentences.
example: ಬಟ್ಟೆ (Batte)=clothes, ಚಪ್ಪಲಿ (Chappal)=slippers. 'Ee batte eshtu?' = How much are these clothes?

action: Learn to read Kannada conjunct consonants (ottakshara). When two consonants combine: ಕ+ಕ=ಕ್ಕ, ಸ+ತ=ಸ್ತ, ನ+ನ=ನ್ನ. Practice reading 10 common conjuncts.
description: Learn conjunct consonants — when two consonants join without a vowel between them. This is the hardest part of Kannada script.
context: Conjuncts appear in very common words: ಅಕ್ಕ (akka), ಅಮ್ಮ (amma), ಕನ್ನಡ (Kannada). You can't read Kannada fluently without recognizing them.
constraint_note: Practice these 10: ಕ್ಕ, ಮ್ಮ, ನ್ನ, ಪ್ಪ, ಲ್ಲ, ಸ್ತ, ದ್ದ, ತ್ತ, ಗ್ಗ, ಜ್ಜ. Write a word containing each.
example: ಅಕ್ಕ (akka=elder sister), ಅಮ್ಮ (amma=mother), ಕನ್ನಡ (Kannada), ಅಪ್ಪ (appa=father), ಇಲ್ಲ (illa=no).

action: Learn 10 place/building words: Angadi (shop), Aaspathre (hospital), Shale (school), Devaalaya (temple), Masjid (mosque), Church, Bank, Post office, Police station, Petrol bunk.
description: Learn 10 place and building words. Combined with direction words, these let you ask for and give directions to anywhere.
context: Place words + 'yelli?' (where?) = navigation. 'Aaspathre yelli?' (Where's the hospital?) could be critical someday.
constraint_note: Make 5 'where is' questions: 'Bank yelli?', 'Shale yelli?'. Then give directions using words you know.
example: ಅಂಗಡಿ (Angadi)=shop, ಆಸ್ಪತ್ರೆ (Aaspathre)=hospital, ಶಾಲೆ (Shale)=school. 'Hattira-dalli angadi ide?' = Is there a shop nearby?

items.body: Pronounced AN-ga-di. Shop/store. 'Angadi-ge hogbeku' = need to go to the shop. 'Batte angadi' = cloth shop. 'Angadi beedhi' = market street. In Bangalore, 'Commercial Street' is still called 'angadi beedhi' by locals. A fundamental word for daily errands.

Pronounced AAS-pat-re. Hospital. 'Aaspathre-ge hogbeku' = need to go to hospital. Borrowed from English 'hospital' but fully Kannadized. 'Aaspathre-yalli' = in the hospital. Knowing this word is important for emergencies — 'hattira aaspathre yelli?' = where is the nearest hospital?

Pronounced SHAA-le. School. 'Shaale-ge hogu' = go to school. 'Shaale magu' = school child. 'Shaale chutti' = school holiday. Education is highly valued in Karnataka — 'shaale' is one of the first words children learn to write. 'Shaale master' = school teacher.

narrator_action: Learn 10 place/building words: Angadi (shop), Aaspathre (hospital), Shale (school), Devaalaya (temple), Masjid (mosque), Church, Bank, Post office, Police station, Petrol bunk.

action: Write the complete Kannada consonant grid from memory: all 5 rows (ka, cha, Ta, ta, pa groups) + semi-vowels and sibilants.
description: Test yourself: write the entire Kannada consonant set from memory. This is the ultimate script recall test.
context: If you can write the full consonant grid from memory, you truly know the Kannada alphabet. This is a milestone.
constraint_note: No peeking. Write all 5 rows of 5, then the 9 additional consonants. Check after. Celebrate what you got right.
example: Row 1: ಕ ಖ ಗ ಘ ಙ. Row 2: ಚ ಛ ಜ ಝ ಞ. Row 3: ಟ ಠ ಡ ಢ ಣ. Row 4: ತ ಥ ದ ಧ ನ. Row 5: ಪ ಫ ಬ ಭ ಮ. Plus: ಯ ರ ಲ ವ ಶ ಷ ಸ ಹ ಳ.

action: Learn numbers 11-20: Hannondu (11), Hannereradu (12), Hadimooru (13), Hadinaalku (14), Hadinaidu (15), Hadinaaru (16), Hadinelu (17), Hadinentu (18), Hattombattu (19), Ippattu (20).
description: Learn Kannada numbers 11-20. These follow a pattern: 'hadi' (ten) + unit number, with some irregularities.
context: Numbers 11-20 are essential for prices, addresses, and ages. Most market prices fall in this range.
constraint_note: Say them in sequence until fluent. Then practice random recall. Notice the pattern: hadi + number.
example: 11=Hannondu, 12=Hannereradu, 15=Hadinaidu, 20=Ippattu. Pattern breaks at 11, 12, 19, 20 — memorize those separately.

action: Learn number multiples: Ippattu (20), Movattu (30), Nalvattu (40), Aivattu (50), Aravattu (60), Eppattu (70), Embattu (80), Tombattu (90), Nooru (100).
description: Learn Kannada tens (20-100). With these + 1-10, you can say any number up to 100.
context: Tens let you handle any price or quantity. 'Aivattu rupaayi' (50 rupees), 'Movattu nimisha' (30 minutes).
constraint_note: Practice counting by tens: 10, 20, 30... 100. Then combine: 'Movattu aidu' (35), 'Aravattu mooru' (63).
example: 20=Ippattu, 50=Aivattu, 100=Nooru. Combine: Ippattu aidu=25, Aivattu mooru=53, Tombattu ombattu=99.

action: Try reading a Kannada shop sign, menu, or newspaper headline. Sound out each letter. Write down what you read and what you think it means.
description: Real-world reading practice. Find any Kannada text around you — a sign, a menu, a product label — and try to read it.
